title: Independent Study
description: (R-6) Offered at Missoula College. Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student.

title: Introduction to Surgical Technology
description: Offered spring. Offered at Missoula College. Restricted to students in the Surgical Technology program. Provides an orientation to the scrub and circulatory roles of the surgical technologist in the preoperative, intraoperative and postoperative periods. Entry level skills and theories are emphasized.

title: Surgical Lab I
description: Offered spring. Offered at Missoula College. Restricted to students in the Surgical Technology program. Demonstration of sterile technique in the campus lab, various skills, and their application in the operating room. Graded CR/NCR.

title: Surgical Pharmacology
description: Offered spring. Offered at Missoula College. Prereq., M 105 or 115 or M 121 or M 151 or M 171 . Restricted to students in the Surgical Technology program. Basic overview of the medications commonly used before, during, and after a surgical procedure.

title: Special Topics
description: (R-9) Offered intermittently. Offered at Missoula College. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one-time offerings of current topics.

title: Operating Room Techniques
description: Offered autumn. Offered at Missoula College. Prereq., AHST 101 , AHST 115 , and AHST 154 . Restricted to students in the Surgical Technology program. Focus is on the scrub and circulator roles of the surgical technologist in the preoperative, intraoperative, and postoperative periods, more complex skills and theories, and the impact of new technologies in the 21st century operating room.

title: Surgical Procedures I
description: Offered autumn. Offered at Missoula College. Prereq., AHST 101 , AHST 115 , and AHST 154 . Restricted to students in the Surgical Technology program. A study of surgical procedures following the patient through the preoperative, intraoperative, and post-operative stages of specific surgical specialties.

title: Surgical Procedures II
description: Offered spring. Offered at Missoula College. Restricted to students in the Surgical Technology program. A study of surgical procedures following the patient through the preoperative, intraoperative, and postoperative stages of specific surgical specialties.

title: Surgical Lab II
description: Offered autumn. Offered at Missoula College. Restricted to students in the Surgical Technology program. Demonstration of more complex skills in the campus lab, including assistant circulating, and their applications in the operating room. Graded CR/NCR.

title: Surgical Clinical I
description: Offered autumn. Offered at Missoula College. Restricted to students in the Surgical Technology program. Perioperative experience in minor surgical procedures through a supervised clinical hospital rotation.

title: Surgical Clinical II
description: Offered spring. Offered at Missoula College. Restricted to students in the Surgical Technology program. Perioperative experience in major surgical procedures through a supervised clinical hospital rotation.

title: Surgical Internship
description: Offered spring. Offered at Missoula College. Restricted to students in the Surgical Technology program. Capstone experience in the perioperative role in preparation for initial employment, increasing occupational awareness and professionalism. Students take call for emergency surgeries alongside experienced hospital staff.

title: Anthropology & the Human Experience
description: Offered autumn and spring. Offered intermittently in summer. A survey of anthropology which introduces the fundamental concepts, methods and perspectives of the field. The description and analysis of human culture, its growth and change. The nature and functions of social institutions.

title: Intro to South & S. East Asia
description: Offered intermittently. An introduction to South and Southeast Asian regions, cultures, societies, and histories, with particular emphasis on artistic, religious and literary traditions from prehistory to the present. Countries include India, Indonesia, Vietnam, Thailand, Sri Lanka, Pakistan and Nepal. An overview approach with different materials and emphases.

title: Intro Latin American Studies
description: Multidisciplinary survey and introduction to Latin America from pre- Columbian times to the present.

title: Ancient Migrations
description: Offered intermittently. An exploration of migrations in human prehistory and history as known from DNA studies, the archaeological record, historical linguistics, the human fossil record and history. How these migrations have impacted the culture, institutions, and biology of contemporary societies and populations.

title: Race and Minorities
description: Offered autumn. Analysis of the development and concept of race as a social category and the processes of cultural change within and between ethnic groups.

title: Anthropology and Global Health
description: Offered intermittently. This course will examine a set of global health problems rooted in rapidly changing social structures, cultural beliefs and practices, and environmental and biological realities that transcend geopolitical and other imagined boundaries.  Students will explore case studies (addressing Ebola, tuberculosis, mental illness, and other topics) and a multidisciplinary literature (including anthropology, epidemiology, history, and biomedicine) centering on how biosocial perspectives might provide a foundation to improve health and well-being on a global level.

title: Food and Culture
description: Offered spring. Examination of the ways culture shapes the satisfaction of a biological need; food production, preparation, choices, customs, taste, taboos, beverages, spices and food distribution around the globe.

title: The Silk Road
description: Offered autumn and spring. Introduction to the study of the human communities, cultures, and economies in Central and Southwest Asia along the ancient four thousand mile-long Silk Road.

title: Archaeology of Yellowstone: 11,000 Years of Native Americans in Yellowstone National Park
description: Offered autumn. Introduces students to the Native American prehistory and history of Yellowstone National Park, from 11,000 years ago to the present. The focus is on archaeological sites in the various areas of the park, including lakes, mountains, obsidian sources, rivers, and geyser basins.

title: Special Topics
description: (R-6) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one-time offerings of current topics.

title: Independent Study
description: (R-6) Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student.

title: Internship
description: Offered intermittently. Extended classroom experience that provides practical application of classroom learning during placements off campus. Prior approval must be obtained from the faculty supervisor. A maximum of 6 credits of Internship (198, 298, 398, 498) may count toward graduation.

title: Anthropology for Majors: Understanding the Four-Field Approach
description: Offered spring. This course is designed as an advanced low-level undergraduate overview of the four-field approach to the study of anthropology. Students will learn about each of the four fields of Anthropology, including Archaeology, Biological Anthropology, Cultural Anthropology, and Linguistics. While this course is introductory, it will employ innovative curriculum for students who are majoring in anthropology. Each semester, faculty will use one or more major anthropology research issues as a guiding matrix for the dissemination of anthropological concepts. The course is intended for low-level undergraduate students either majoring in anthropology or considering a major in the discipline.

title: Anthropological Statistics
description: An introduction to the statistical methods and reasoning used by anthropologists. Course requires used of a Windows or Macintosh computer and will utilize free statistics software the student must run on their computer.

title: Introduction to Biological Anthropology
description: Offered autumn. An introduction to human evolutionary biology including processes of evolution, primate studies, hominin paleontology, and human variation.

title: Biological Anthropology Lab
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., or corequisite ANTY 210N . This lab course allows students to more deeply explore the concepts and materials covered in Introduction to Biological Anthropology. Students will engage in lab based activities involving human genetics and processes of evolution, biology and behavior of non-human primates, human evolution, and modern human adaptation and variation.

title: Culture & Society
description: Offered autumn and spring. Study of social organization of non-western societies; emphasis on variations in ecology, social structure, economic, political and religious beliefs and practices.

title: Central Asian Culture and Civilization
description: Offered autumn even numbered years. Introduction to Central Asia's history, culture and ways of thinking. Focus on the political and social organization of Central Asia and cultural changes as expressed in art and interactions with China, India and the Middle East.

title: Intro to Archaeology
description: Offered autumn and spring. What archaeologists do and how they reconstruct past human cultures. Methodological and theoretical approaches to understanding and explaining past human societies.

title: Foundations of Civilization
description: Focus on the worldwide evolution of human society from Stone Age hunter-gatherers to the beginnings of modern civilization. Approached through the colorful and exciting world of archaeologists and the sites they excavate.

title: Archaeological Wonders of the World
description: Offered spring even numbered years. Prereq., WRIT 101 or WRIT 201 . This course highlights the classical civilizations of the ancient world, fields such as Egyptology and Classical Archaeology, and the major archaeological discoveries which are associated with them.

title: Special Topics
description: (R-6) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one-time offerings relating to current problems or new developments in the discipline.

title: Human Variation
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., ANTY 210N . Introduction to human biological variation, and to the methods and theories that are used to explain the distribution of variable features.

title: Human Evolution
description: Offered autumn, odd-numbered years. Prereq., ANTY 210N . An exploration of the fossil and archaeological records of the evolution of human beings, and of current methods and theories used in interpreting these data.

title: Principles of Forensic Anthropology
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., ANTY 210N .  A study of techniques for recovering skeletal material, identifying and interpreting human skeletal remains, keeping records, interacting with the law enforcement system and documenting human rights abuses.

title: Native Peoples of Montana
description: Offered spring. The history and culture of the Indian tribes in Montana.

title: Indigenous Peoples & the Ethics of Development
description: Offered spring odd-numbered years. This class will examine the impact of global development on tribal and Indigenous peoples. Discussion topics will include ethical issues, land, health, employment, and cultural change caused by global development as well as explore how and why Indigenous societies resist or adapt to imposed changes.

title: Peoples and Cultures of World
description: (R-9) Offered autumn and spring. Study of the peoples of various geographic regions and their cultures.

title: Myth, Ritual and Religion
description: Offered intermittently. Theories and practices concerning supernatural phenomena, and the comparative study of world religions and cosmological traditions of indigenous peoples throughout the world.

title: Central Asia and Its Neighbors
description: Offered intermittently. Analysis of the human communities and cultures of Central and Southwest Asia, with particular emphasis on the importance of relationships with neighboring countries and civilizations since ancient times.

title: Archaeology of North America
description: Offered autumn. The origins, backgrounds and development of Pre-Columbian American peoples and cultures.

title: Archaeology of Montana
description: Offered spring. The origins, distributions and development of aboriginal cultures in Montana and surrounding regions.

title: PaleoIndian Archaeology
description: Offered spring or winter, even-numbered years. Examines archaeological, linguistic, biological and skeletal data to determine from where and when Native Americans arrived in North America.  Examines archaeological sites from such diverse places as Montana, Siberia, Virginia, and Chile to answer the most intriguing question in contemporary American archaeology today:  how, when and from where did people first arrive in the Americas?

title: Mesoamerican Prehistory
description: Offered spring odd-numbered years. The development of civilization and prehistoric states in the New World. Prehistoric lifeways and the effects of European contact on these cultures.

title: Special Topics
description: (R-9) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one-time offerings of current topics.

title: Internship
description: (R-6) Offered intermittently. Extended classroom experience that provides practical application of classroom learning during placements off campus. Prior approval must be obtained from the faculty supervisor. A maximum of 6 credits of Internship (198, 298, 398, 498) may count toward graduation.

title: History of Anthropology
description: Offered intermittently. Restricted to majors and minors in Anthropology. The development of theory and method in cultural anthropology to the present. Various archaeological, ethnological and socio-psychological theories in the light of historical anthropology. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Anthropological Data Analysis
description: Restricted to Anthropology majors and minors. A general introduction and survey of anthropological research methods to conduct fieldwork, data collection, and data analysis, including ethical issues and decision-making in the profession and public presentation of research. Level: Undergraduate/Graduate.

title: Public Anthropology
description: Offered spring odd-numbered years. Prereq., ANTY 101H or ANTY 220S . Focus on the public accountability, transparency and the value and effects of anthropological research for local, national, and transnational communities. Level: Undergraduate

title: Anthropological Museology
description: Offered spring even-numbered years. Introduction to anthropological museums, museum work and museum theory. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Contemporary Issues of Indigenous Peoples
description: Offered autumn. An examination of the major issues that affect the contemporary experiences of Indigenous Peoples of the Americas, and other global communities. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Preceptorship in Anthropology
description: (R-6) Offered autumn and spring. Prereq., ANTY 210N or ANTY 220S or ANTY 250S . Assisting a faculty member by tutoring, grading objective exams, conducting review sessions, and carrying out other class-related responsibilities. Open to juniors, senior, and graduate students with consent of the faculty member with whom they serve. Proposals must be approved by department chair. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Osteology
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., ANTY 314 . A detailed examination of the human skeleton with an emphasis on identifying individual bones and their structures. Specifically extended to fragmentary skeletal elements. Direct hands-on experience required. Level: Undergraduate/Graduate

title: Forensic and Mortuary Archaeology
description: Offered intermittently. Prereq., ANTY 314 . Practical approaches to locating, documenting and recovering human skeletal remains, including surface scatters and burials. Emphasis on interpretations of evidence for recovery scene formation and mortuary behavior. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Forensic Anthropology and Bioarchaeology
description: Offered autumn.  Prereq., ANTY 210N and either CJUS 125N or CJUS 488 .  An exploration of the principles, theories, and applications of forensic anthropology and bioarchaeology.  The focus is on how information about a person’s identity and the lifeways of the society in which they lived is inferred from human remains in the criminal investigation context and the archaeological context. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Emergence of Modern Humans
description: Offered intermittently. Prereq., ANTY 210N . An exploration of the emergence of "modern" humans and their relationships with Neanderthals. Exploration of what it means to be "a modern human" through an examination of human evolutionary history. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Evolution and Genetic Variation in Human Populations
description: Offered intermittently. Prereq. ANTY 310 . Human genetic variation examined from a molecular perspective. Emphasis on the role of infectious disease and other factors as a selective factor in human evolution and exploration of the implications of these associations for human genetic variation. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Mind, Culture, and Society
description: Offered intermittently. The study of socialization, personality, cognition, and mental health cross-culturally. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Culture and Identity
description: Offered intermittently. The comparative study of identity formation along and across racial, ethnic, and ethno-national lines. Emphasis on issues of ethnogenesis, cultural resistance, transformation, domination, colonialism as well as sharing to understand both the cultural commonalties and differences in identity formation. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Anthropology of Gender
description: Offered intermittently. Comparative study of the history and significance of gender in social life. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Social Anthropology
description: Offered intermittently. Prereq., WRIT 101 or WRIT 201 . Seminar style senior capstone course for cultural anthropology students. This course focuses on bringing theory and methods together in written and visual ethnography. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Ethnographic Field Methods
description: Offered intermittently. Prereq., WRIT 101 or WRIT 201 . Introduction to socio-cultural anthropological methods including participant observation, interviewing and narrative techniques and analysis of qualitative data. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Indigenous Global Health & Healing
description: The course examines traditional and contemporary uses of medicinal plants and spiritual healing within Indigenous North American societies. The class also examines the impact Indigenous people's knowledge of medicinal plants has had on non-Indigenous people's health as well as the field of medicine. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Cities and Landscapes of Central Asia
description: Offered intermittently. Analysis of the main centers of civilization and culture, rich sites and monuments of Central Asia and Southwest Asia since ancient times. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Artistic Traditions of Central Asia
description: Offered intermittently. Analysis of the study of human artistic creativity and scientific innovations of various cultures in Central and Southwest Asia since ancient times. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Archaeological Theory
description: Offered autumn odd-numbered years. Prereq., WRIT 101 or WRIT 201 ; and ANTY 250S . Historical trends and current major theories and methods in archaeology. Co-convenes with ANTY 550 . Level: Undergraduate.

title: Cultural Resource Management
description: Offered autumn, odd-numbered years and intermittently in summer. Introduction to the laws and practice of cultural resource/heritage property management. Focus on the management of archaeological sites, historic structures, and traditional cultural places due to federal laws. Emphasis is on laying foundation of CRM practices for students interested in pursuing it as a potential career. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: GIS in Archaeology
description: Offered intermittently. Prereq., ANTY 250S . Anthropological and archaeological data acquisition, management, and analysis using Geographic Information Systems (GIS) tools and techniques. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Lithic Technology
description: Offered autumn odd-numbered years. Analysis of stone artifacts and debitage. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Artifact Analysis
description: Offered spring. Prereq., WRIT 101 or WRIT 201 ; and ANTY 250S . Laboratory approaches and techniques for analyzing material culture from technological, stylistic, and chronological perspectives. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Historical Archaeology
description: Offered spring. Prereq., ANTY 250S . Understanding and interpreting the past through historical archaeological remains, methods, and theories. Focuses on historical archaeological sites and topics from the American West, but also examines the field’s global perspective. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Archaeology of Hunter-Gatherers
description: Offered autumn even-numbered years. Introduction to the archaeological study of hunter-gatherer societies. Primary emphasis on archaeological method and theory. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Archaeology of the Arctic and Subarctic
description: Introduction to the study of Arctic and Subarctic archaeology emphasizing the Pleistocene and Holocene prehistory of North America and eastern Siberia. Understanding of methodological problems associated with archaeology in a northern context, the evolution of Inuit, Eskimo, Aleut and Athapaskan cultures, and hunter-gatherer adaptations to northern interior and coastal environments. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Archaeology of the Southwest United States
description: The development of the prehistoric communities in the southwestern United States from ancient times to the dawn of history in the area. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Archaeological Survey
description: (R-12) Prereq., ANTY 250S . Offered autumn. A field course in Montana archaeology. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Archaeological Field School
description: (R-12) Offered summer and/or winter. Prereq., ANTY 250S . Provides students with a well-rounded experience in archaeological field methods.  Field schools will typically occur at archaeological site locations away from campus.  During the archaeological field experience, students may learn methods of excavation, survey, research, and analysis to facilitate their transition to careers as professional archaeologists. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Methods for Native Languages
description: (R-6) Offered spring. In an effort to highlight promising methodologies that will advance the success of Native language acquisition and instruction, students will be exposed to an innovative methodology while being instructed in an Indigenous language. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Special Topics
description: (R-9) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one-time offerings of current topics. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Independent Study
description: (R-6) Offered every term. Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Seminar/Workshop
description: (R-9) Offered intermittently. A review and discussion of current research. Topics vary.

title: Field Experience/Practicum
description: (R-12) Offered intermittently. Organized field experience or language practicum in anthropology. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Internship
description: (R-6) Practical application of classroom learning through internship in a number of areas such as museology, cultural resource management and forensics. Level: undergraduate.

title: Contemporary Anthropological Thought
description: Offered intermittently. Restricted to graduate students in Anthropology. A review of major contributions to current anthropological theory, with an emphasis on the application of theory to anthropological problems. Significant advances in general theory, symbolic anthropology, critical theory, cultural studies, and postmodernism. Level: Graduate

title: Applied Anthropological Statistics
description: Offered spring odd-numbered years. Restricted to graduate students in Anthropology. A review of simple and multivariate, descriptive, and inferential statistical methods and their application to the students’ own thesis or dissertation data. Course requires use of a Windows or Macintosh computer and will use free software that students must run on their computers.

title: Seminar on Human Variation & Evolution
description: (R-6) Offered autumn. Various topics related to genetic evidence of human biological evolution, morphological and genetic diversity of modern humans, and problems of "race". Level: Graduate

title: Advanced Forensic Anthropology
description: (R-6) Offered spring. Review of traditional methods and exploration of new methods of skeletal analysis, as applied to cases from the forensic collection. Level: Graduate

title: Seminar in Bioarcheaology & Skeletal Biology
description: (R-6) Offered spring. Theoretical and methodological approaches to the analysis of human skeletal remains derived from archaeological contexts. Demography, health and disease, diet and nutrition, growth, activity patterns, and measures of biological relatedness are interpreted within a biocultural framework. Level: Graduate

title: Theory & Methods in Bioanthropology
description: Offered autumn.  A detailed review of the body of theory that is foundational for the study of human evolution, human variation, bioarchaeology, forensic anthropology, and primatology, along with a consideration of major methods used to analyze data in these fields. Level: Graduate

title: Seminar in Contemporary Ethnography
description: Offered intermittently. A review and discussion of current ethnographic research. Level: Graduate

title: Seminar in Archaeology
description: Offered autumn odd-numbered years. A review and discussion of current research.Topics vary. Co-convenes with ANTY 450 . Level: Graduate

title: Seminar Historical Archaeology
description: Offered autumn odd-numbered years. An exploration of theories, methods, and literature in historical archaeology. Level: Graduate

title: Evolutionary Archaeology
description: Offered autumn even-numbered years. Examination of method and theory in Darwinian evolutionary archaeology. Seminar assignments and discussions focus on human behavioral ecology, cultural transmission, and macroevolution. Level: Graduate

title: Research
description: (R-10) Offered every term. Directed individual research and study appropriate to the background and objectives of the student. Level: Graduate

title: Special Topics
description: (R-24) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one-time offerings of current topics. Level: Graduate

title: Independent Study
description: (R-9) Offered every term. Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student. Level: Graduate

title: Seminar/Workshop
description: (R-9) Offered intermittently. A review and discussion of current research. Topics vary.

title: Internship
description: (R-6) Offered intermittently. Restricted to graduate students in Anthropology. Practical application of classroom learning through internship in a number of areas such as museology, cultural resource management and forensics. Written reports are required. Level: Graduate

title: Thesis/Professional Project
description: (R-10) Offered every term. Restricted to graduate students in Anthropology. Preparation of a thesis, manuscript, or professional project based on research for presentation and/or publication. Level: Graduate

title: Issues in Cultural Heritage
description: Offered autumn. Doctoral dissertation research activities. A review of the range of topics that fall under the umbrella of cultural heritage and a review of theory and practice in one or more of these topics. Level: Graduate

title: Research Design & Proposal Preparation
description: Offered spring. Restricted to graduate students in Anthropology. Covers the development of anthropological research designs and proposals. Level: Graduate

title: Cultural Heritage Policy & Practice
description: Offered spring odd-numbered years. Restricted to graduate students in Anthropology. Exploration of critical issues in cultural heritage policy emphasizing the regulatory basis for federal CRM, public anthropology, and indigenous people's issues. Hands-on training in the design and production of federal planning documents. Level: Graduate

title: Advanced Research
description: (R-6) Offered every term. Directed individual research and study appropriate to the background and objectives of the student. Level: Graduate

title: Seminar in Cultural Heritage
description: (R-6) Offered intermittently. A review and discussion of current research.  Topics vary. Level: Graduate

title: Dissertation
description: (R-10) Offered every term. Doctoral dissertation research activities. Level: Graduate

title: Internship
description: Offered intermittently. Extended classroom experience that provides practical application of classroom learning during placements off campus. Prior approval must be obtained from the faculty supervisor. A maximum of 6 credits of Internship (198, 298, 398, 498) may count toward graduation.

title: Elementary Modern Standard Arabic I
description: Offered autumn. This course brings students the opportunity to learn Arabic via a communicative approach, where the emphasis is placed on the functional use of the Arabic Language. Active skills are listening, speaking, reading, and writing, plus basic cultural study.

title: Elementary Modern Standard Arabic II
description: Offered spring. Prereq., ARAB 101 . A continuation of ARAB 101 . Students learn Arabic via a communicative approach, where the emphasis is placed on the functional use of the Arabic Language. Active skills are listening, speaking, reading, and writing, plus basic cultural study.

title: Special Topics
description: (R-6) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one–time offerings of current topics.

title: Intermediate Modern Standard Arabic I
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., ARAB 102 . This course is designed to help students further develop their language skills (listening, speaking, reading, and writing) and learn more about Arab culture and advanced grammar rules beyond the elementary level. The target proficiency level is intermediate-low/mid based on proficiency guidelines from the American Council on the Teaching of Foreign Languages.

title: Intermediate Modern Standard Arabic II
description: Offered spring. Prereq., ARAB 201 . This course is a continuation of ARAB 201 . It is designed to help students further develop their language skills (listening, speaking, reading, and writing) and learn more about the Arab culture and advanced grammar rules beyond the elementary level. The target proficiency level is intermediate-mid based on proficiency guidelines from the American Council on the Teaching of Foreign Languages.

title: Special Topics
description: (R-8) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one–time offerings of current topics.

title: Independent Study
description: (R-6) Offered autumn and spring. Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student.

title: Advanced Modern Standard Arabic I
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., ARAB 202 . This course is designed to help students further develop their language skills (listening, speaking, reading, and writing) and learn more about Arab culture and advanced grammar rules beyond the intermediate level. The target proficiency level is intermediate-high based on proficiency guidelines from the American Council on the Teaching of Foreign Languages.

title: Advanced Modern Standard Arabic II
description: Offered spring. Prereq., ARAB 301 . This course is a continuation of ARAB 301 . It is designed to help students further develop their language skills (listening, speaking, reading, and writing) and learn more about Arab culture and advanced grammar rules beyond the intermediate level. The target proficiency level is intermediate-high/advanced-low based on proficiency guidelines from the American Council on the Teaching of Foreign Languages.

title: The Arab World
description: Offered autumn. Students explore the Arabic-speaking countries through in-depth discussions of their history, geography, peoples, economy, political systems, educational systems, and cultural components, such as music, cuisine, tradition, customs, gender relations, etc. This course is designed for everyone interested in the topic, and no previous knowledge of the Arabic language is required.

title: Special Topics
description: (R-9) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one–time offerings of current topics.

title: Independent Study
description: (R-12) Offered autumn and spring. Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student.

title: Visual Language - Drawing
description: Offered autumn and spring. Introduction to basic drawing skills and concept integration. Studio practice with research in historical and contemporary approaches.

title: Visual Language - 3-D Foundations
description: Offered autumn and spring. Introduction to the formal elements and principles of design in 3-D.

title: Ceramics for Non-majors
description: Offered Offered autumn and spring. A general introduction to ceramics for non-art majors. Learn a variety of techniques working with clay and glazes. No Art experience required. Not for B.A, B.F.A majors or art minors.

title: Special Topics
description: (R-12) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings by visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, and/or one-time offerings of current topics.

title: Seminar
description: (R-6) Offered intermittently. A review and discussion of current research. Topics vary.

title: Drawing I
description: Offered intermittently. Prereq., ARTZ 105A . Study of human anatomy through drawing, in-class skills development, homework portfolio, and research in historical and contemporary figuration required.

title: Illustration
description: Offered autumn. This course is an introduction to drawing techniques as they pertain to the commercial illustration industry; emphasis on creative interpretation and disciplined draftsmanship for the visual communication of ideas.

title: Painting I
description: Offered autumn and spring. Prereq., ARTZ 105A . Introduction to acrylic and oil painting. Emphasis on color theory, composition, concept development, and research in historical and contemporary strategies.

title: Ceramics I
description: Offered autumn and spring. Introduction to ceramic techniques. Introduction to clay as a historical and contemporary medium to explore art making. Emphasis on handbuilding and wheel throwing, and concept development including large scale sculpture and functional pottery.

title: Sculpture I
description: Offered autumn and spring. Offered autumn and spring. Introduction to fundamental technical skills and processes used to make sculptural objects. Emphasis on formal concerns, concept development, and new technologies in art.

title: Printmaking I
description: (R-9) Offered autumn and spring. Emphasis on multiples, layering color, and collaboration. Topics may include: relief, intaglio, lithography, screenprinting, artist books, mixed media, or photo-processes. Consideration of historical and contemporary approaches.

title: Photography I: Technologies and Processes
description: Offered autumn and spring. Introduction to photography. Emphasis on exposure, digital camera basics, composition, digital photography processing, and print finishing techniques. Consideration of historical and contemporary approaches.

title: Special Topics
description: (R-12) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings by visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, and/or one-time offerings of current topics.

title: Teaching Early Childhood and Elementary Art
description: Offered autumn and spring. Restricted to majors in Elementary Education, Early Childhood Education, and Art Education. This is a general teaching methods course in visual art education. This course is designed to provide students with a fundamental understanding of the roles, values, classroom pedagogy, and current issues in P-8 visual art education; to investigate child development; to offer an introduction to content standards; and to examine a broad array of art education theories and practices. Students are introduced to available curriculum resources and will engage in academic service learning.

title: Drawing II
description: (R-12) Offered intermittently. Prereq., ARTZ 211A . Focus on integrating content and form in drawings and research in historical and contemporary ideas.

title: Environmental Drawing
description: (R-6) Offered intermittently. Open to students in the Wilderness and Civilization program (Wilderness Studies minor) during fall semesters. Open to all students during spring semester. Exploration and production of drawings, with an emphasis on an environmental context; studio practicum, lectures, critiques, reading and writing required. Some restrictions might be applied to specific sections of this course.

title: Painting II
description: (R-12) Offered autumn and spring. Prereq., ARTZ 221A . Continued development of painting skills and concepts with an emphasis on contemporary ideas and approaches. Topics may include: figuration, place, process, abstraction, and other contemporary themes.

title: Ceramics II
description: (R-12) Offered autumn and spring. Prereq., ARTZ 231A . More advanced development of ceramic process with emphasis on idea development and individual skill building. Depending on the section handbuilding, wheel throwing and other technique will be taught. Focus on integrating content and form through study of historical and contemporary approaches.

title: Clay and Glaze
description: Offered autumn. In-depth study of the physical and chemical properties of clays and glazes. Hands-on testing of clay and glaze formulas and introduction to kiln ﬁring.

title: Sculpture II
description: (R-12) Offered autumn and spring. Continued development of sculptural processes. Emphasis on clear sculptural responses to material-based and topic-based assignments. Consideration of both analog and digital means. Topics may include fabrication, foundry, fiber, and other materials and approaches.

title: Printmaking II
description: (R-12) Offered autumn and spring. Continued development of printmaking processes. Emphasis on integration of content. Focus on layering of color, increased scale, styles of format and presentation, and research in historical and contemporary approach topics may include: relief, screenprinting, monotypes, book arts, and outsourced digital fabrication methods.

title: Data Arts
description: Offered autumn or spring. Data Arts teaches the essential and practical skills necessary to communicate information about data clearly and effectively through graphic and visual design.

title: Analog Photography
description: Prereq., ARTZ 284A or JRNL 257A . This is an exploration of analog photographic techniques and methods employing light-sensitive film and paper as well as using a variety of different camera systems and film formats.  The course emphasizes advancement of technical control and darkroom experimentation resulting in students establishing individual portfolios. A 35 mm film camera is required for this class.

title: Photography II: Theory, Criticism, and Practice
description: (R-12) Offered autumn and spring. Prereq., ARTZ 284A or JRNL 257A . Further exploration of digital photography. Emphasis on effective use of color, advanced camera operation and image editing techniques with software and image output, studio and strobe lighting. Emphasis on content development, technical knowledge, presentation, and research in historical and contemporary approaches.

title: Alternative Process Photography
description: Offered intermittently. Prereq. or coreq., ARTH 250L and ARTZ 284A . Exploration of historic and alternative photography techniques such as cyanotype, pinhole, and wet-plate. Focus on digital negative, historic optics, and varied approaches to format, presentation, and research in historical and contemporary approaches.

title: Synthesis
description: Offered spring. Prereq., or corequisite ARTZ 380 . This course allows students to draw together their learning and their critical thinking into a defining experience, through the development of a synthesis project and a written presentation.

title: Special Topics
description: (R-12) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings by visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, and/or one-time offerings of current topics.

title: Seminar
description: (R-6) Offered intermittently. Topics vary.

title: Art Practicum/Field Experience
description: (R-12) Supervised, practical experience in a professional setting relevant to the student's field of study.

title: Internship
description: (R-12) Offered autumn and spring. Prereq., ARTH 250L . Extended classroom experience that provides practical application of classroom learning during placements off campus. Prior approval must be obtained from the faculty supervisor. A maximum of 6 credits of Internship (198, 298, 398, 498) may count toward graduation.

title: Teaching Art I - K-12
description: Offered fall. Restricted to Art majors. This course is designed to provide students with a fundamental understanding of the roles, values, classroom pedagogy, and current issues in K-12 visual art education; to investigate child development; to offer an introduction to content standards; and to examine a broad array of art education theories and practices. Students are introduced to curriculum resources and will engage in academic service learning. Level: Undergraduate/Graduate

title: Teaching Art II - K-12
description: Offered spring. Prereq., ARTZ 402 . Restricted to Art majors. Continuation and practical application of ARTZ 402 . Level: Undergraduate/Graduate

title: Advanced Research- Drawing
description: (R-9) Offered intermittently. Advanced Research Drawing, Further exploration of time-based studio practice including assignments and student proposals. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Advanced Research- Painting
description: (R-9) Offered intermittently. Prereq., ARTZ 351 . Further exploration and establishment of a functional studio art practice including technical skills, concept development, and understanding of contemporary themes and theory. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Advanced Research- Ceramics
description: (R-9) Offered intermittently. Prereq., ARTZ 231A . An advanced critique-based studio class where students design their own project proposals and work one-on-one with the faculty. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Undergraduate Research
description: (R-12) Independent research under the direction of a faculty member. Level: undergraduate.

title: Special Topics
description: (R-12) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings by visiting professors, new courses, and/or one-time offerings of current topics.

title: Independent Study
description: (R-12) Offered intermittently. Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Seminar- Professional Practices
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., ARTH 250L . Restricted to students with senior standing. Required of all graduating B.F.A. students. Introduction to professional practices and standards in the visual arts, including: presentation, portfolio development, career and exhibition opportunities, arts advocacy, and graduate school application. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Senior Studio Project
description: Offered spring. Capstone studio class of a self-determined ambitious thesis project. Specific projects formats are determined individually and include thematic integration. Examples of possible formats include a series of artworks, website videos, books, illustrations, collaborative multi-disciplinary events, multi-media presentations in other professions, data visualizations, etc. Level: undergraduate.

title: Presentation to the Community
description: (R-2) Offered spring. This course examines the practical aspects of bringing a creative project forward to the public. Students will develop all aspects associated with staging a presentation of their final portfolio, including: writing proposals, press releases, CV and artist statements; developing budgets, seeking funding, installing artwork, and documenting presentation. Level: undergraduate.

title: Internship
description: (R-12) Offered intermittently. Extended classroom experience that provides practical application of classroom learning during placements off campus. Prior approval must be obtained from the faculty supervisor. A maximum of 6 credits of Internship (198, 298, 398, 498) may count toward graduation. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Senior Thesis/Capstone
description: Offered spring. Prereq., ARTZ 494 . Restricted to students with senior standing. Requires successful passage of the B.F.A. review. This required course for B.F.A. students focuses on completion of artwork and preparation for required spring B.F.A. exhibition. Further exploration of professional practices topics and career opportunities. Level: undergraduate.

title: Graduate Critique Seminar
description: (R-16) Offered autumn and spring. Restricted to graduate students in Art. Weekly meetings to critique graduate student work. Level: Graduate

title: Pre-Candidacy
description: (R-2) Offered autumn and spring. Restricted to graduate students in Art. Taken concurrently with 500-level Graduate Research/Studio Processes. Emphasis on one-on-one instruction with faculty in preparation for review prior to thesis work. Level: Graduate

title: Graduate Studio Research: Art
description: (R-24) Offered spring. Restricted to graduate students in Art. Regular meetings with faculty to discuss development of individual work. Level: Graduate

title: Graduate Pedagogy
description: Offered autumn. Restricted to graduate students in Art. This course focuses on current pedagogical practices in college-level art education. Emphasis is on the syllabus, teaching philosophy, assignment preparation, public-speaking skills development, TA application, and course shadowing. Level: Graduate

title: Beyond Art School
description: Restricted to graduate students in Art. Introduction to professional practices and standards in the visual arts. Emphasis is on portfolios, resumes, web development, exhibition opportunities, grant writing, and artist residencies. Level: Graduate

title: Expanded Studio Practice
description: (R-6) Offered autumn and spring. Restricted to graduate students in Art. Studio-based course with emphasis on collaborative practices across media. May include topic-based studio assignments, thematic inquiry, responses to readings, and/or specific studio processes. Level: Graduate

title: Graduate Topics in Painting
description: (R-9) Offered intermittently. Exploration of studio art painting including concept development, and understanding of contemporary themes and theory. Level: Graduate

title: Data Arts
description: Offered autumn and spring. This course teaches the essential and practical skills necessary to communicate information about data clearly and effectively through graphic and visual design. Level: Graduate

title: Special Topics
description: (R-12) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings by visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, and/or one-time offerings of current topics. Level: Graduate

title: Independent Study
description: (R-18) Offered intermittently. Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student. Level: Graduate

title: Seminar
description: (R-6) Offered intermittently. A review and discussion of current research. Topics vary. Level: Graduate

title: Internship
description: (R-6) Offered intermittently. Restricted to graduate students in Art. Extended classroom experience that provides practical application of classroom learning during placements off campus. Prior approval must be obtained from the faculty supervisor. Level: Graduate

title: Thesis Paper/Exhibition
description: Offered intermittently. One-on-one instruction with thesis committee chair and thesis exhibition preparation. Level: Graduate

title: Introduction to Art History
description: Surveys world art from prehistory through the present day with the objective of developing a critical understanding of art forms in their historical and cultural context.

title: Special Topics
description: (R-12) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings by visiting professors, new courses, and/or one-time investigations of current topics.

title: Art of World Civilization: 1400 to Present
description: Offered spring. Survey of the history of visual art from 1400 to the present.

title: Introduction to Art Criticism
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., WRIT 101 or WRIT 201 ; and ARTH 150H . Study of the description, interpretation, and evaluation of visual art as practiced in art criticism.

title: Special Topics
description: (R-12) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings by visiting professors, new courses, and/or one-time investigations of current topics.

title: Architectural History I
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., ARTH 150H or ARTH 201H . Exploration of architectural styles, designs, and choices of the built environment from prehistory to the modern age.

title: Architectural History II
description: Prereq., ARTH 201H and ARTH 250L . Exploration of architectural styles, designs, and choices of the built environment from 1850 to the present.

title: Edconic: Contemporary Art & Global Feminisms
description: Offered every semester. Restricted to students in the Edconic program. Taught at Sotheby's Institute in London by Edconic instructors. This course will consider the key global currents of fifty years of feminist art around a number of themes. These include feminist art about the body and sexuality, women's domestic labour, feminist approaches to identity, motherhood and childcare, violence against women, feminism, and the art historical canon. Students will encounter these themes through a number of different media, from painting, photography and sculpture to textile, digital, and performance art.

title: Edconic: Hogarth to Himid: Histories of British Art
description: Offered every semester. Restricted to students in the Edconic program. Taught at Sotheby's Institute in London by Edconic instructors. This course provides a survey of British art spanning from the 17th century to the present day, introducing students to artworks, artists, and significant collections of British art. In the breadth of art and artists studied, there will be particular attention given to Black and multicultural British art and women artists who have been less visible in canonical surveys of British art.

title: Edconic: Curator Academy
description: Offered every semester. Restricted to students in the Edconic program. Taught at Sotheby's Institute in London Edconic instructors. This course surveys current developments in curating art, examining expanded definitions of curating (in museums and independently post-2020), and the evolving local, global, and digital landscapes for curatorial work and activity today.

title: Edconic: The Global Art Market
description: Offered every semester. Restricted to students in the Edconic program. Taught at Sotheby's Institute in London by Edconic instructors. This course explores the emergence and key concepts of the global market for art from the 18th to the 21st centuries through a discussion of its history, disruptions and innovations. The course takes a thematic approach delivered via specific case studies to map the key concepts, individuals and institutions, and the various business models, and ethical and legal considerations that underpin the contemporary market.

title: Edconic: Exploring Collections, Exploding Canons
description: Offered every semester. Restricted to students in the Edconic program. Taught at Sotheby's Institute in London by Edconic instructors. This course introduces and examines London's internationally significant museum and gallery collections from a critical and global perspective. Exploring the politics of collecting and display, it engages with contemporary art historical and curatorial debates via the realities of institutions and their collections - and the inherent tensions therein.

title: Contemporary Art and Art Criticism
description: Offered autumn and spring. Prereq., WRIT 101 or WRIT 201 ; and ARTH 250L . Exploration of artists, art works, critics, and theories from 1960 to the present.

title: Manga and Anime: History, Practice, and Form
description: Surveys the history and genres of Japanese comics (manga) and animation (anime). Students will learn how to analyze/draw the visual elements of manga and anime while also learning about cultural context, production, and reception. In addition to discussing common themes and narrative forms employed, students will learn to distinguish among, describe, and draw the various art styles and framing structures.

title: Special Topics
description: (R-12) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings by visiting professors, new courses, and/or one-time investigations of current topics.

title: Baroque Art
description: Offered intermittently. Prereq., ARTH 150H or ARTH 201H and ARTH 250L . Exploration of European art and architecture from 1600 to 1700. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Contemporary American Indian Art
description: Offered intermittently. Prereq., ARTH 150H . Provides an overview of American Indian artwork with an emphasis on contemporary artwork from the United States and tribes that spanned the US-Canada border. Studies Native American artistic traditions, and discusses issues and themes particular to Indigenous artwork. Explores the cultural, political, social, and historic contexts for the artwork, providing students with a multidisciplinary understanding of Indigenous artwork. Prioritizes Indigenous voices as a direct source of knowledge and insight. Level: Undergraduate/Graduate

title: 20th Century Art
description: Offered intermittently. Prereq., ARTH 150H or ARTH 201H and ARTH 250L . Exploration of art and architecture of the 20th century. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Open Range: Land Art and the American Consciousness
description: Offered spring. The aim of this course is to investigate the sense of place in American visual arts of the 19th to 21st centuries. The course provides a roughly chronological view of the development of place in the American landscape, particularly in the west, and how that has been articulated by visual artists. Level: undergraduate.

title: History of Photography
description: Offered intermittently. Prereq., ARTH 150H or 201H and ARTH 250L . Exploration of the history of photography from the early 19th century to the 21st century. Level: undergraduate.

title: Advanced Research in Art History
description: (R-9) Offered autumn and spring. Further exploration of art history topics including student proposals. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Special Topics
description: (R-9) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings by visiting professors, new courses, and/or one-time investigation of current topics. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Independent Study
description: (R-6) Offered intermittently. Further exploration of studio practice including technical and conceptual student proposals. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Seminar
description: (R-9) Upper-division seminar in varying topics of art history and criticism. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Internship
description: (R-12) Offered intermittently. Prereq., ARTH 250L . Extended classroom experience that provides practical application of classroom learning during placements off campus. Prior approval must be obtained from the faculty supervisor. A maximum of 6 credits of Internship (198, 298, 398, 498) may count toward graduation. Level: undergraduate and graduate.

title: Critical Theories in the Visual Arts I
description: Seminar on the history of art criticism. Level: Graduate

title: Critical Theories in Visual Arts II
description: Prereq., ARTH 503 . Continuation of ARTH 503 with an emphasis on contemporary theories and topics. Level: Graduate

title: Graduate Architectural History I
description: Offered autumn. This course offers students a knowledge and understanding of architecture styles, designs, and choices of the built environment from prehistoric megalithic architecture to the start of the modern age.

title: Graduate History of Architectural Design II
description: Offers students a knowledge and understanding of architecture styles, designs and choices of the built environment of the modern age. Level: Graduate

title: Museum Studies Seminar
description: (R-12) Offered autumn, spring, and summer. This seminar emphasizes the practical knowledge, skills, and abilities in Museum Studies.

title: Research in Art History
description: (R-24) Offered intermittently. Research for graduate students in art history and/or studio. Graded credit/no-credit. Level: Graduate

title: Special Topics
description: (R-6) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one-time offerings of current topics. Level: Graduate

title: Independent Study
description: (R-6) Offered intermittently. Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student. Level: Graduate

title: Museum Studies Field Project
description: Offered autumn, spring, and summer. Graduate students enrolled in the Museum Studies Certificate program will take this course to polish and present their year-long practical Field Projects to the core faculty.

title: Internship
description: (R-6) Offered intermittently. Extended classroom experience that provides practical application of classroom learning during placements off campus. Prior approval must be obtained from the faculty supervisor.

title: Thesis
description: (R-6) Preparation of a thesis or manuscript based on research for presentation and/or publication. Level: Graduate

title: Planetary Astronomy
description: Offered autumn. Students should have taken high school algebra and geometry prior to registration. An introduction to observational, historical, and planetary astronomy.  Students will have a chance to visit UM's state-of-the-art planetarium and observe with our 0.4 meter telescope.

title: Stars, Galaxies, and the Universe
description: Offered spring. Students should have taken high school algebra and geometry prior to registration. An introduction to stars, stellar evolution, galaxies, and cosmology. Students will have a chance to visit UM’s state-of-the-art planetarium and observe with our 0.4 meter telescope.

title: Planetary Astronomy Lab
description: Offered autumn. Prereq. or coreq., ASTR 131N . Laboratory exercises in observational and planetary astronomy.  Students will have a chance to visit UM's state-of-the-art planetarium and observe with our 0.4 meter telescope.

title: Stars, Galaxies, and the Universe Lab
description: Offered spring. Prereq. or coreq., ASTR 132N . Laboratory exercises in observational, stellar, and galactic astronomy.  Students will have a chance to visit UM’s state-of-the-art planetarium and observe with our 0.4 meter telescope.

title: The Evolving Universe
description: Offered spring. Students should have a working knowledge of precalculus (ie. college algebra and college trigonometry) prior to registration. Overview of recent developments in planetary system formation, stars, galaxies, and cosmology. Some astronomical observing will be required outside of normal class hours.

title: Special Topics
description: (R-6) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses or one-time offerings of current topics.

title: Internship
description: (R-6) Offered intermittently. Extended classroom experience which provides practical application of classroom learning during placements off campus. Prior approval must be obtained from the faculty supervisor. A maximum of 6 credits of Internship (198, 298, 398, 498) may count toward graduation.

title: Independent Study
description: (R-9) Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student.

title: Planetary and Exoplanet Science
description: Offered autumn even-numbered years. Prereq., PHSX 215N / PHSX 216N or PHSX 205N / PHSX 206N ; and M 162 or M 171 . Physical and geological characteristics of planets, satellites, asteroids, comets, and meteoroids, with an emphasis on comparative planetology.

title: Galaxies and Cosmology
description: Offered spring odd-numbered years. Prereq., ASTR 132N or ASTR 142N , and PHSX 217N - PHSX 218N or PHSX 207N - PHSX 208N , and M 273 . The nature of the Milky Way galaxy and other galaxies, galactic evolution, the large scale structure of the universe, active galaxies and quasars, and cosmology, including the early universe.

title: Observational Astronomy
description: Offered autumn even-numbered years. Prereq., ASTR 132N or ASTR 142N , and PHSX 207N - PHSX 208N or PHSX 217N - PHSX 218N . Laboratory study of the probabilistic behavior of light, data acquisition with telescopes, digital imaging and spectroscopy. Emphasis on fundamental statistical tools, scientific computer programming, and written and oral presentation of scientific results.

title: Stellar Astronomy & Astrophysics I
description: Offered autumn odd-numbered years. Prereq., ASTR 132N or ASTR 142N , and PHSX 207N - PHSX 208N or PHSX 217N - PHSX 218N. Prereq. or coreq., M 273 . Detailed application of physical laws to determine the nature of the stars; analysis of stellar spectra and atmospheres; solar astrophysics; structure of stars and their evolution.

title: Stellar Ast & Astrophys II
description: Offered spring even-numbered years. Prereq., ASTR 363 . Continuation of ASTR 363 .
