title: Internship
description: (R-6) Offered intermittently. Extended classroom experience which provides practical application of classroom learning during placements off campus. Prior approval must be obtained from the faculty supervisor. A maximum of 6 credits of Internship (198, 298, 398, 498) may count toward graduation. Level: Undergraduate

title: Capstone
description: Offered autumn. Restricted to students with junior or senior standing in Physics. Capstone project on research performed prior to or during the student's senior year. Level: Undergraduate.

title: Emergency Management in Athletic Training
description: Restricted to students in the Master of Athletic Training program. Serves as an introduction to athletic training practice. Emphasis on the prevention, care, and management of acute injuries and illnesses, as well as risk management, environmental concerns, and protective taping and equipment. Level: Graduate

title: Foundations of Health Care in Athletic Training
description: This course presents the overarching framework, principles, and core responsibilities of athletic training practice working in multidisciplinary healthcare teams. The course also provides the necessary foundation for athletic training practice focusing on patient-centered care, interprofessional practice, evidence-based practice, quality improvement and healthcare informatics. Level: Graduate

title: Foundations of Research and Evidence Based Practice in Athletic Training
description: This course explores quantitative and qualitative research methodologies used in athletic training and evaluation of published research in the field. The course will introduce evidence-based practice research, provide strategies for reading and writing critically, and offer a plan to applying evidence-based practice in daily clinical practice. Level: Graduate

title: Practicum in Athletic Training I
description: Restricted to students in the Master of Athletic Training program. Builds on skills previously acquired and introduces new skills related to current coursework. Students will be assigned to a clinical education rotation under the direct supervision of a clinical preceptor. First in the series of four practicum courses. Level: Graduate

title: Practicum in Athletic Training II
description: Restricted to students in the Master of Athletic Training program. Expands on skills previously acquired and introduces new skills related to current coursework. Students will be assigned to a clinical education rotation under the direct supervision of a clinical preceptor. Second in the series of four practicum courses. Level: Graduate

title: Orthopedic Assessment I
description: Provides a study of anatomy and physiology, assessment, evaluation techniques, treatment, and management of conditions affecting the lower extremities and lumbar spine. Level: Graduate

title: Diagnosis and Management of Sports Related Concussion
description: This course discusses assessment techniques commonly used in the clinical evaluation of sport related concussion. The course also provides the necessary foundation for establishing inter-professional care teams to treat sport related concussion. Level: Graduate

title: General Medical Assessment
description: Restricted to students in the Master of Athletic Training program. Examines the recognition, assessment, and management of general medical conditions and illnesses. Level: Graduate

title: Orthopedic Assessment II
description: Provides a study of anatomy and physiology, assessment, evaluation techniques, treatment, and management of conditions affecting the upper extremities, head, and thoracic and cervical spine. Level: Graduate

title: Practicum in Athletic Training III
description: Restricted to students in the Master of Athletic Training program. Broadens skills previously acquired and introduces new skills related to current coursework. Students will be assigned to a clinical education rotation under the direct supervision of a clinical preceptor. Third in the series of four practicum courses. Level: Graduate

title: Practicum in Athletic Training IV
description: Restricted to students in the Master of Athletic Training program. Reviews and refines skills previously acquired and evaluated in previous coursework. Students will be assigned to a clinical education rotation under the direct supervision of a clinical preceptor. Fourth in the series of four practicum courses. Level: Graduate

title: Clinical Immersion in Athletic Training I
description: Practice intensive experience that allows the student to experience the totality of care provided by athletic trainers. Students will be assigned to a clinical education rotation under the direct supervision of a clinical preceptor. This is the first in the series of three immersion courses. Level: Graduate

title: Clinical Immersion in Athletic Training II
description: Practice intensive experience that allows the student to experience the totality of care provided by athletic trainers. Students will be assigned to a clinical education rotation under the direct supervision of a clinical preceptor. This is the second in the series of three immersion courses. Level: Graduate

title: Clinical Immersion in Athletic Training III
description: Practice intensive experience that allows the student to experience the totality of care provided by athletic trainers. Students will be assigned to a clinical education rotation under the direct supervision of a clinical preceptor. This is the third in the series of three immersion courses. Level: Graduate

title: Clinical Anatomy Laboratory
description: Offered Fall. Prereq., Athletic Training Student. Clinical applications of anatomy in Athletic Training. Laboratory time for practical applications including prosected cadavers, surface anatomy, osteology, radiology, functional analysis of movement, applied clinical anatomy and sports application. Level: Graduate

title: Manual Therapy Techniques
description: Offered summer. Prereq., ATEP 572. Theories and application methods of comprehensive manual therapy for athletic injuries. Level: Graduate

title: Performance and Technology in Athletic Training
description: Grounded in return-to-competition decision making, the course material covers all aspects of sport performance including nutrition and methods of physical training. Technologies are integrated in movement analyses to help quantify athletic movements and support clinical decisions. Level: Graduate

title: Pharmacology for Sports Medicine
description: Explores the pharmaceutical and chemical processes of therapeutic interventions and therapies. This course examines the constraints placed on patients in the performance environment as well management, protocols, and legal issues. Level: Graduate

title: Therapeutic Interventions I
description: Theories and application methods of comprehensive therapeutic treatment, modalities and rehabilitation programs for injuries commonly sustained by the physically active to the lower extremity. The first course in a series of three courses. Level: Graduate

title: Therapeutic Interventions II
description: Theories and application methods of comprehensive therapeutic treatment, modalities and rehabilitation programs for injuries commonly sustained by the physically active to the upper extremity. The second in a series of three courses. Level: Graduate

title: Therapeutic Interventions III
description: Theories and application of methods of comprehensive manual therapy and joint manipulation techniques for the treatment of athletic injuries. Level: Graduate

title: Healthcare Administration and Leadership in Athletic Training
description: Exploration of the aspects of athletic training leadership styles, organization and administration. This course focuses on program leadership, risk management, policy and procedure development, and organizational operations. Level: Graduate

title: Research
description: This course explores quantitative and qualitative research methodologies used in athletic training and evaluation of published research in the field, including but not limited to: quality improvement projects, novel research, case studies, and systematic reviews. The first course of this series involves developing and completing a practice-based research project, culminating in the creation and submission of research abstracts. Level: Graduate

title: Seminar in Athletic Training
description: This course provides an opportunity for students to engage in conversations with clinical experts focused on transition to clinical practice. Level: Graduate

title: Research Capstone in Athletic Training
description: This course explores quantitative and qualitative research methodologies used in athletic training and evaluation of published research in the field, including but not limited to: quality improvement projects, novel research, case studies, and systematic reviews. The second course of this series involves disseminating a practice-based research project, culminating in the creation of a final written document and presentations. Level: Graduate

title: Introductory Biology for Biochemists
description: Prereq., CHMY 141N & CHMY 142N . Prereq. or coreq., CHMY 143N & CHMY 144N . Coreq., BCH 111 .  An introductory course that explores biomolecules and their roles in life processes.  Provides a foundation for Cellular and Molecular Biology ( BIOB 260 ), Genetics and Evolution ( BIOB 272 ), Introductory Biochemistry Seminar ( BCH 294 ), and many other advanced science courses.

title: Introductory Biology for Biochemists Lab
description: Prereq., CHMY 141N & CHMY 142N . Prereq. or coreq., CHMY 143N & CHMY 144N . Coreq., BCH 110 . Introduction to the experimental techniques used to study biomolecules and their roles in life processes.  Provides a foundation for other advanced level laboratory courses in chemistry and biochemistry.

title: Seminar/Workshop
description: Offered spring. Prereq., CHMY 143N . An introduction to important advances in biochemistry through readings from the primary literature and discussion of this literature. Faculty members will also make presentations on their research. Graded credit/no credit.

title: Biochemistry
description: Offered autumn and spring. Prereq., CHMY 223 or BIOB 260 .  Fundamental biochemistry; chemistry and metabolism of biomolecules, energy relationships in metabolism; storage, transmission, and expression of genetic information.  Credit not allowed for both BCH 380 and 480 -482.

title: Advanced Biochemistry I
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., CHMY 223 . Primarily for science majors. The chemistry of biomolecules, with emphasis on the structure and function of proteins, carbohydrates, lipids and nucleic acids. The chemistry and regulation of the transfer and expression of genetic information, protein synthesis. Credit not allowed for both BCH 380 and 480 -482. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Advanced Biochemistry II
description: Offered spring. Prereq., BCH 480 . Continuation of BCH 480 . Enzyme kinetics, metabolism, especially macromolecule biosynthesis and energy acquisition pathways, and the associated energetics and molecular physiology. Credit not allowed for both BCH 380 and BCH 480 -482. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Biochemistry Research Lab
description: Offered spring. Prereq., BCH 380 or BCH 480 . Restricted to students in the Biochemistry or Chemistry majors or minors. Other students will be allowed to enroll on a space-available basis with the consent of the instructor. Applications of biochemical principles to modern protein biochemistry. Basic micro- and molecular-biology techniques are used to produce mutant proteins; then students learn basic and advanced biophysical techniques to characterize the mutant proteins. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Undergraduate Research
description: (R-10) Offered every term. Independent research under the direction of a faculty member. Level: Undergraduate

title: Special Topics
description: (R-10) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one-time offerings of current topics. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Internship/Cooperative Ed
description: (R-15) Extended non-classroom experience that provides practical application of classroom learning during placements off campus. Prior approval must be obtained from the faculty supervisor. A maximum of 6 credits of Internship (198, 298, 398, 498) may count toward graduation. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Senior Thesis/Capstone
description: (R-6) Offered every term. Restricted to students with senior standing. Preparation of a thesis or manuscript based on undergraduate research for presentation and/or publication. Student must give an oral or poster presentation at the Undergraduate Research Symposium or a scientific meeting. Level: Undergraduate

title: Experimental Molecular, Cellular, and Chemical Biology
description: (R-14) Same as BIOB 547 . Focus on experimental design, methods, and presentation of experimental results for graduate students in laboratories with a molecular, cellular or chemical biological focus. Level: Graduate

title: RNA Structure & Function
description: (R-8) Offered every semester. Prereq., BCH 482 & BIOB 260 . Exploration of current scientific literature and new data that focuses on RNA biochemistry. Emphasis on literature relevant to research on RNA viruses and ribosomes and protein synthesis. Level: Graduate

title: Intro to Research
description: (R-2) Offered autumn and spring.  Required course for Biochemistry and Biophysics graduate students. Students are acquainted with faculty research projects. Instruction in basic research techniques, research equipment. Introduction to relevant scientific research literature.  Level: Graduate

title: Physical Biochemistry
description: Offered spring odd-numbered years. Techniques of physical chemistry used in studying biological structure and function of macromolecules. Emphasis is on spectroscopic methods, hydrodynamic methods and x-ray and other scattering and diffraction techniques. Level: Graduate

title: Proteins and Enzymes
description: Offered autumn even-numbered years. Prereq., BCH 482 . An investigation into the structure/function relationship in proteins and a detailed exploration of enzyme kinetics, using examples from current literature. Level: Graduate

title: Nucleic Acids
description: Offered autumn odd-numbered years. Prereq., BCH 482 . Emphasis on critical reading of current literature that investigates structure, chemistry, and function of nucleic acids. Level: Graduate

title: Research
description: (R-40) Offered autumn and spring. Directed individual research and study appropriate to the background and objectives of the student. Level: Graduate

title: Special Topics
description: (R-24) Offered intermittently. Requires graduate standing and consent of instructor. Experimental offerings of new courses by resident or visiting faculty. Level: Graduate

title: Thesis
description: (R-10) Offered intermittently. Restricted to students in the Biochemistry and Biophysics M.S. program. Laboratory research for and preparation of a master's thesis. Level: Graduate

title: Cell Organization & Mechanisms
description: Offered spring even-numbered years. Prereq., BCH 480 . Primary literature exploration of the regulation of structure, function, and dynamics of eukaryotic cells. Topics include membranes, cytoskeleton, transcription, translation, signal transduction, cell motility, cell proliferation, and programmed cell death. Level: Graduate

title: Biochemistry & Biophysics Seminar
description: (R-14) Credit/No credit only. Presentation of current research in Structural Biology, Biochemistry, Biophysics, or related fields by invited outside speakers, UM faculty, and senior graduate students. Level: Graduate

title: Dissertation
description: (R-20) Offered intermittently. Restricted to doctoral students in Biochemistry & Biophysics. Laboratory research for and preparation of a doctoral dissertation. Level: Graduate

title: Introductory Ecology
description: Offered spring.  An introduction to ecological principles, stressing the structure and function of natural communities and examining human's role in these ecosystems.

title: Field Ecology
description: Offered summers only at the Flathead Lake Biological Station. Students taking this course should have completed one semester of college-level biology, chemistry, and mathematics. The principles and practices of the study of animals and plants in their natural environments, including human influences, with focus on the Crown of the Continent area of the Rocky Mountains and taught entirely outdoors.

title: General Ecology
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., BIOB 272 . Analysis of the distribution and abundance of plants and animals. Includes individual, population and community-level processes (e.g., population growth and regulation, competition, predation, succession, nutrient cycling, energy flow and community organization).

title: General Ecology Lab (equivalent to 271)
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., BIOB 161N , WRIT 101 , BIOE 370 , and either STAT 216 or WILD 240 . BIOE 370 may be taken prior to or simultaneously with this course. Methods of describing and testing alternative explanations for patterns in nature. The use of scientific methodology in ecology.

title: Seminar/Workshop
description: Offered autumn.  Preparatory readings and attendance at seminars on a wide variety of ecological and wildlife management topics followed by critiques.

title: Aquatic Microbial Ecology
description: Offered summer only at the Flathead Lake Biological Station. Students taking this course should have completed one semester of college-level biology and chemistry and two semesters of college-level mathematics. This course is an immersive (2 week) summer class offered to both undergraduate and graduate students with interests in microbiology and ecology. The course includes lectures, laboratories, and several field-based sampling trips. The course provides conceptual foundation and hands-on field and laboratory training in modern methods in aquatic microbial ecology. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Comparative Vertebrate Anatomy
description: Offered intermittently. Prereq., BIOB 260 or BIOB 272 . Comparative study of vertebrate morphology using evolutionary transitions among species to explore design and function. Laboratory includes systematic study of organ systems and workshops in the dynamics of biomechanics and functional morphology. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Behavior & Evolution
description: Offered autumn, odd-numbered years. Prereq., BIOB 272 . Diversity of animal  behavior in an evolutionary context including inheritance of behavior, diets, avoidance responses, mating systems and sexual selection, parental care, and evolution of animal groups and societies. Level: Undergraduate

title: Alpine Ecology
description: Offered summers only at the Flathead Lake Biological Station. Students taking this course should have completed one semester of college-level biology, chemistry, mathematics, and ecology. Distribution, abundance and life cycles of plants and animals and their unique ecophysiological adaptations to life in the rigorous environments of the high mountains above the timberline, with emphasis on the Crown of the Continent area. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Freshwater Ecology
description: Offered spring. Prereq., BIOB 160 .  Physical and chemical dynamics of lakes and streams. Diversity, distribution and dynamics of freshwater organisms. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Stream Ecology
description: Offered summers only at the Flathead Lake Biological Station. Students taking this course should have completed two semesters of college-level biology and one semester of college-level mathematics, chemistry, and ecology. The biota and biogeochemical processes of running waters with unifying principles and contemporary research approaches. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Conservation Biology
description: Offered summers only at the Flathead Lake Biological Station. Students taking this course should have completed one semester of college-level biology and ecology. Concepts and approaches for sustaining biodiversity and other natural goods and services provided by terrestrial and aquatic systems. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Ecosystem Ecology
description: Offered spring. Prereq., BIOB 160 or BIOB 170N or BIOO 105N or BIOE 172N . Introduction to systems thinking and the ecosystem concept, review of water and energy balances, carbon cycling, nutrient cycling, trophic dynamics, and species effects on ecosystem function across terrestrial and aquatic ecosystems. Level: Undergraduate

title: Terrestrial Plant Ecology
description: Offered autumn, even-numbered years. Prereq., BIOE 172N or BIOE 342 or BIOE 370 or FORS 330 . The interrelationships between plants and plant communities and their natural environment. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Landscape Ecology
description: Offered summers only at the Flathead Lake Biological Station. Students taking this course should have completed two semesters of college-level biology and mathematics and one semester of college-level ecology and chemistry. Biophysical processes that determine landscape and ecosystem structure and function using remote sensing tools, geographic information systems and dynamic models to demonstrate landscape change. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Lake Ecology
description: Offered summers only at the Flathead Lake Biological Station. Students taking this course should have completed two semesters of college-level biology and one semester of college-level chemistry, mathematics, and ecology. The physical, chemical and biological characteristics of lake ecosystems with an emphasis on nutrient cycling, food web interactions and water quality. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Forest and Fire Ecology
description: Offered summers only at the Flathead Lake Biological Station. Students taking this course should have completed two semesters of college-level coursework (sophomore standing). Patterns, processes, and disturbances of northern Rocky Mountain forests in the context of principles of population, community, landscape, and ecosystem ecology with particular emphasis given to ecology of wildfire. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Plant Evolution
description: Offered autumn, odd-numbered years. Prereq., BIOB 272 and either BIOO 335 (preferred ) or BIOO 320 . Lecture, reading and discussion on the evolutionary processes that shape major patterns of plant diversity.  Topics include but are not restricted to:  local adaptation, floral and mating system evolution, polyploidy, genome evolution, and speciation. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Advanced Undergrad Research
description: (R-10) Offered every term. Restricted to students with junior or senior standing. Independent research under the direction of a faculty member. Graded credit/no credit. Level: Undergraduate

title: Seminar
description: (R-8) Offered intermittently. Presentations by student, faculty, and associates on issues and topics in their field. Level: Graduate

title: Discover Biology
description: Offered every term. Offered on Mountain Campus and at Missoula College. Contemporary exploration of the organization and complexity of living organisms and the systems in which they live. The central questions of biology -- the relationship between form and function, acquisition and use of energy, and continuity between generations will be addressed through lectures and laboratory investigations.

title: Montana Ecosystems
description: Offered autumn and spring. Offered at Missoula College. An introduction to the landscapes and ecosystem diversity of Montana, with an emphasis on exploring the dominant habitats of western Montana. Required, integrated laboratory includes field trip investigations, classroom lab exercises, and presentations.

title: Apprentice-Level Beekeeping
description: This course aims to teach the basic beekeeping of honey bees. Participants will gain a general knowledge of honey bee biology and how to care for honey bees throughout the year. Students will be taught how to recognize common honey bee ailments and pests and the methods for treating them. After completion, students will know how to manage honey bee colonies for maximum bee health and honey production as well as a general understanding of basic insect biology.

title: Principles of Living Systems
description: Offered every term. Non-science majors are encouraged to take either BIOB 101N (Discover Biology) or BIOB 170N (Principles of Biological Diversity) instead of BIOB 160 . Unifying principles of biological structure-function relationships at different levels of organization and complexity. Consideration of reproduction, genetics, development, evolution, ecosystems, as well as the inter-relationships of the human species to the rest of life. Students requiring a laboratory should also register for BIOB 161N .

title: Principles of Living Systems Lab
description: Offered autumn and summer. Coreq. or prereq., BIOB 160 . Lab experiences illustrate biological principles underlying growth, reproduction, development, genetics and physiology, and are designed to give students practice in scientific methods of description, development of hypotheses, and testing.

title: Principles of Biological Diversity
description: Offered spring. Survey of the diversity, evolution and ecology of life including prokaryotes, viruses, protista, fungi, plants and animals.

title: Principles of Biological Diversity Lab
description: Offered spring. Prereq. or Coreq., BIOB 170N . The diversity of life including prokaryotes, viruses, protista, fungi, plants and animals including structure and evolutionary relationships.

title: Special Topics
description: Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one-time offerings of current topics.

title: Your Future in Biology
description: Your Future in Biology is a one-credit seminar that will introduce students to the diverse set of careers that are available with a biology degree. Interactions with the instructor and visiting speakers will highlight the suite of skills that a degree in biology provides and that are required to tackle crucial challenges in healthcare, genomics, biotechnology, environmental science, conservation, and other biology-focused careers. Students will directly engage with world-class researchers and successful professionals using their training in biology to solve important problems and to provide essential services to society. Along the way, speakers will share their personal stories and academic journeys to help students better understand the many pathways to success with a degree in biology. Students will also interact regularly with members of the Division of Biological and Biomedical Sciences, including the Associate Dean, to help them establish meaningful and lasting connections at UM. Graded credit/no-credit only.

title: Internship
description: (R-6) Extended classroom experience that provides practical application of learning during placement off campus. Prior approval must be obtained from the faculty supervisor. A maximum of 6 credits of Internship (198, 298, 398, 498) may count toward graduation.

title: Communicating Biology
description: Offered autumn and spring. Prereq., WRIT 101 or WRIT 201 . Offered at Missoula College. An examination of modern methods for sharing scientific discovery with an emphasis on biological issues related to the human experience.

title: Our Environment & Us
description: This course approaches the topic of environmental ethics from a biology perspective. We will discuss the specific environmental consequences of human behavior as well as their potential ethical implications. It is largely a readings and writings class, although some lecture material will be presented, and assigned readings and thoughtful written responses will constitute a significant portion of the coursework. The class is divided into four units. In the first unit, we will explore concepts related to the value of the natural world: anthropocentrism, instrumental and intrinsic value, moral importance of species, environmental stewardship, and ecological sabotage. The second unit will delve into the ethics involved in our interaction with animals: animal moral worth, well-being, and the morality of animal use in research, meat consumption, factory farming, and animal captivity for conservation and education. The third unit centers on climate change topics: the grave complexity of biological consequences, strategies such as the polluter-pays approach, personal responsibility, indigenous viewpoints, technological solutions, and rejection of climate science. In the last unit, we will cover issues related to future human generations: ethical considerations of having kids, excess consumption, population growth, and what is the moral value of unborn generations.

title: Journeyman-Level Beekeeping
description: Prereq., BIOB 125 . This course will cover topics such as the honeybees’ interaction and relation to native pollinators and the plants they work with as well as a look into pesticide uses and truths. The student will be given detailed instruction on the honeybees’ internal anatomy and structures and the uses of their unique external anatomy. Students will learn to use the microscope and understand how it is used in diagnosing insect diseases and pests. By the end of the course, students will understand the honeybees’ place in the ecosystem and how their unique anatomy contributes to their interactions with the world both inside and outside the hive.

title: General Science: Chemical & Life Sciences
description: Offered spring. Prereq. or coreq., M 132 . Integrated lectures, laboratory exercises, and field trips on topics in chemical and biological science for prospective elementary school teachers and the non-scientist. Two two-hour laboratory sessions are required each week.

title: Cellular and Molecular Biology
description: Offered autumn and summer. Prereq., BIOB 160 (preferred) or BCH 110 / BCH 111 (preferred) or B- or higher in BIOH 112 ; and either CHMY 123 or CHMY 143N . Analytical exploration of the structure and function of the cell, the fundamental unit of life, with an emphasis on energy transformations and information flow. Topics include molecular building blocks, membranes, organelles, and mechanisms of replication, gene expression, metabolism, signal transduction, cell birth, cell death, and cell differentiation.

title: Genetics and Evolution
description: Offered spring. Prereq., either BIOB 260 ; OR both BIOB 160 and BIOB 170N ; OR just BIOB 160 with a B- or better; AND one of M 121 , 122 , 151 , 162 , or 171; OR Maplesoft Pre-Calculus>=10; OR Maplesoft Algebra>=17; OR EdReady Math placement level 4. Principles and mechanisms of inheritance and evolution. Population genetics, fossil record, macroevolution, speciation, extinction, systematics, molecular evolution.

title: Natural Beekeeping: Integrating Honey Bee Ecology into Modern Beekeeping
description: Prereq., BIOB 125 . This course introduces a method of beekeeping that, as best we can, emulates how bees naturally occur in nature while promoting the practices that qualify us as beekeepers, not just people with a hive in the backyard. This course is designed to introduce the beekeeper to a method of beekeeping that incorporates the behaviors and preferences of naturally nesting colonies into managed beekeeping practices.

title: Special Topics
description: (R-6) Offered intermittently.  Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one-time offerings of current topics.

title: Student Teaching
description: (R-6) Offered intermittently. Offered at Missoula College. Organized student teaching.

title: Internship
description: Offered intermittently. Extended classroom experience that provides practical application of  learning during placement off campus. Prior approval must be obtained from the faculty supervisor. A maximum of 6 credits of Internship (198, 298, 398, 498) may count toward graduation.

title: Developmental Biology
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., BIOB 260 . BIOB 272 is recommended. An analysis of the origin and development of form and patterns in organisms, stressing the processes of growth and differentiation in plants and animals. Graded traditional letter grade only.

title: Master-Level Beekeeping
description: Prereq., BIOB 225 . The Master course is the third and final level in the Master Beekeeping curriculum. It offers a more detailed look into bee flight, anatomy, and reproduction, discusses bee pheromones and genetics, and provides templates for record-keeping for improved bee management. The course also covers hive products, bee nutrition, selection of apiary locations, and basic principles for conducting your own experiments. By the end of the course, students will have a better understanding of advanced bee management and an introduction to the beekeeping business. They should be ready to develop and maintain healthy hives for hobbyists and larger-scale endeavors.

title: General Genetics
description: Offered spring. Prereq., BIOB 260 and 272 . This course will focus on the molecular genetics of eukaryotes, with special emphasis on transmission genetics and gene structure and regulation.

title: Undergraduate Research
description: (R-10) Offered every term. Independent research under the direction of a faculty member. Graded credit/no credit.

title: Special Topics
description: (R-10) Offered intermittently.  Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one-time offerings of current topics.

title: Independent Study
description: (R-10) Offered every term. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one-time offerings of current topics.

title: Practicum
description: (R-12) Organized field experience in biology.

title: Internship
description: (R-6) Offered every term. Extended classroom experience that provides practical application of learning during placement off campus. Prior approval must be obtained from the faculty supervisor. A maximum of 6 credits of Internship (198, 298, 398, 498) may count toward graduation.

title: Immunology
description: Offered autumn.  Prereq., BIOB 260 . Current concepts and methods in Immunology. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Immunology Laboratory
description: Offered autumn. Prereq. or coreq., BIOB 410 . Modern techniques for analysis of immune responses. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Advanced Cellular & Molecular Biology
description: Offered spring. Prereq., BIOB 260 and BIOB 272 . BCH 380 or BCH 480 is strongly recommended. Cell structure and function, cell cycle, cellular signaling, molecular basis of cancer, regulated cell death, membrane transport, organelle dynamics, cytoskeleton, cell adhesion, and the molecular basis of learning and memory. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Comparative Animal Physiology
description: Offered Spring. Prereq., BIOB 260 . Animal physiology with emphasis on diversity of functional processes, with strong links to broader ecological and evolutionary contexts. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Molecular Analysis of Development
description: Offered alternate spring semesters. This course covers key topics in developmental biology through the detailed study of the primary literature. Seminar topics are updated for each year the course is offered and listed in syllabus. With help of the instructor, the students present each topic and lead a discussion each class period based on the assigned research paper and one or two review articles to provide background on the topic. CR/NCR only (no letter grade). Level: Undergraduate

title: Endocrinology
description: Offered intermittently. Prereq., BIOB 260 and BIOB 272 . Integration of fundamental concepts of endocrinology (such as hormone release, hormone transport and receptor activation) into complex systems (such as reproduction). Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Conservation Genetics
description: Offered spring. Prereq., BIOB 272 . Genetic basis for solving biological problems in conservation including the genetics of small populations, the application of molecular genetic techniques to conservation biology and case studies of the application of genetics to conservation problems. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Phylogenetics and Evolution
description: Offered autumn semester, odd-numbered years. Prereq., BIOB 260 and BIOB 272 . Phylogenies, or evolutionary trees, provide insights into the history of life on Earth, including our own origins. This course focuses on the theoretical foundations of popular methods of reconstructing phylogenies from molecular sequence data and how to implement these methods with computational software for real data sets. Other current methods for testing evolutionary hypotheses with sequence data will also be introduced. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Genomics
description: Offered spring. Prereq., BIOB 272 . Principles and mechanisms of genome biology of animals and microbes, including genome function, evolution, and basic molecular and computational methodology used in genome biology. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Advanced Undergraduate Research
description: (R-10) Offered every term. Restricted to students with junior or senior standing. Independent research under the direction of a faculty member. Graded credit/no credit. Level: Undergraduate

title: Special Topics
description: (R-10) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one-time offerings of current topics. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Independent Study
description: (R-10) Offered every term. Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Seminar in Biology
description: (R-3) Offered intermittently. A review and discussion of current research. Topics vary. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Practicum
description: (R-12) Organized field experience in biology. Level: Undergraduate and Graduate

title: Internship
description: (R-6) Offered every term. Extended classroom experience that provides practical application of learning during placement off campus. Prior approval must be obtained from the faculty supervisor. A maximum of 6 credits of Internship (198, 298, 398, 498) may count toward graduation. Level: Undergraduate

title: Undergraduate Thesis
description: (R-6) Offered every term.  Restricted to students with senior standing. Preparation of a thesis or manuscript based on undergraduate research for presentation and/or publication.  Student must give oral or poster presentation at the Biological Sciences Undergraduate Research Symposium or a scientific meeting. Level: Undergraduate

title: OBE Core Course - Genetics and Evolution
description: Offered every other autumn. Exploration of the fundamental concepts and approaches in evolutionary biology, functional biology and evolutionary genetics with evolutionary ecology woven throughout. Lectures and discussions, with an emphasis on primary literature, classic and contemporary. Level: Graduate

title: OBE Core Course - Ecology
description: Offered alternate years. Broad overview of population and community ecology. Lectures and discussions, introducing theoretic foundations and exploring classic and more recent empirical tests of ecological theory with relevant topics in evolutionary ecology and functional biology woven throughout. Level: Graduate

title: Plant-Consumer Interactions
description: Offered alternate years. Prereq., BIOE 370 . Ecology and evolution of plant-consumer interactions.  Review of classic and contemporary literature on plant-consumer interactions. Level: Graduate

title: Readings in Morphology, Physiology, and Zoology
description: (R-8) Review and discussion of current literature in the fields of morphology, physiology, and ecology. Level: Graduate

title: Physiological Plant Ecology
description: Offered alternate years. Prereq., BIOE 370 and BIOO 433 . The physiological basis of plant adaptation and response to the environment. Level: Graduate

title: Electron Microscopy Lab
description: (R-6) Practical laboratory experience in the preparation of various samples and hands-on operation of the transmission and/or scanning electron microscopes. Level: Graduate

title: Experimental Molecular, Cellular, and Chemical Biology
description: (R-14)  Focus on experimental design, methods, and presentation of experimental results for graduate students in laboratories with a molecular, cellular or chemical biological focus. Level: Graduate

title: Environmental Field Study
description: (R-3) Prereq. or coreq., ENSC 540 or ENST 560 . Designing, executing, and interpreting environmental studies. Project oriented. Level: Graduate

title: Population Genetics Seminar
description: (R-12) Current topics in population genetics, evolutionary biology, molecular evolution and related topics. Level: Graduate

title: Molecular Analysis of Development
description: Offered alternate spring semesters. This course covers key topics in developmental biology through the detailed study of the primary literature. Seminar topics are updated for each year the course is offered and listed in syllabus. With help of the instructor, the students present each topic and lead a discussion each class period based on the assigned research paper and one or two review articles to provide background on the topic. Graded CR/NCR only. Level: Graduate

title: Research
description: (R-12) Directed individual research and study appropriate to the background and objectives of the student. Level: Graduate

title: Special Topics
description: (R-22) Experimental offering of new courses by resident or visiting faculty. Level: Graduate

title: Independent Study
description: (R-8) Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student. Level: Graduate

title: Seminar in Biology
description: (R-6) A review and discussion of current research in biology. Topics vary. Level: Graduate

title: Internship
description: (R-8) Extended classroom experience that provides practical application of learning during placement off campus. Prior approval must be obtained from the faculty supervisor. Level: Graduate

title: Thesis
description: (R-10) Field and laboratory research and the writing of a student's master's thesis. Level: Graduate

title: Special Topics
description: (R-24) Experimental offering of new courses by resident or visiting faculty. Level: Graduate

title: Dissertation
description: (R-20) Credit for field and laboratory research and the writing of a student's doctoral dissertation. Level: Graduate

title: Basic Human Biology
description: Offered autumn and spring. Missoula College. A one semester course with laboratory focusing on learning scientific principles and a general understanding of human functional anatomy and physiological processes.

title: Basic Human Biology Laboratory
description: Missoula College. Basic human biology lab is required laboratory for students enrolled in BIOH 104N . Lab activities provide hands-on, investigative instruction in the fundamentals of anatomy and physiology of the human body. Exploration of anatomy using models, preserved materials and demonstrations reinforces principals taught in accompanying lecture.

title: Human Form and Function I
description: Offered autumn. Explores the fundamentals of structure and function at basic cellular and tissue levels, in addition to the anatomy and physiology of the integumentary, musculoskeletal, and nervous systems.

title: Human Form and Function II
description: Offered spring. Explores the fundamental structures and functions of the endocrine, cardiovascular, respiratory, digestive, urinary and reproductive systems.

title: Special Topics
description: (R-6) Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one-time offerings of current topics.

title: Human Anatomy & Physiology I
description: Offered autumn and spring. Offered at Missoula College. Restricted to students in the following majors: Missoula College Health Professions and Pre-Health Professions majors, Medical Information Technology, Pre-Physical Therapy, Athletic Training, Integrative Physiology, and Health & Human Performance. Introductory science course or college-prep high school biology course recommended.  Comprehensive knowledge of human form and function necessary for students preparing for health-related professions. Emphasis on structure, function  and homeostatic regulation of body systems with presentation of basic concepts in chemistry and microbiology as they relate to human anatomy and physiology. Covers tissues through nervous system. Required, integrated laboratory includes some dissection.

title: Human Anatomy and Physiology I Lab
description: Offered autumn and spring. Offered at Missoula College. Coreq., BIOH 201N . Restricted to students in the following majors: Missoula College Health Professions and Pre-Health Professions majors, Medical Information Technology, Pre-Physical Therapy, Athletic Training, Integrative Physiology, and Health & Human Performance. Gen Ed Attributes: Natural Science Lab Course (N)Required companion laboratory for BIOH 201N . Comprehensive study of tissues and gross human anatomy necessary for students in health-related programs.  Emphasis on typical structure and function as they relate to health. Topics include tissues through nervous system. Laboratory activities include some dissection and study of human cadaver.

title: Human Anatomy and Physiology II
description: Offered autumn and spring. Offered at Missoula College. Prereq., BIOH 201N . Coreq., BIOH 212N . Restricted to students in the following majors: Missoula College Health Professions and Pre-Health Professions majors, Medical Information Technology, Pre-Physical Therapy, Athletic Training, and Health & Human Performance. Comprehensive knowledge of human form and function necessary for students in health-related programs. Emphasis on structure function and homeostatic regulation of body systems with presentation of basic concepts in chemistry and microbiology as they relate to human anatomy and physiology. Covers endocrine through reproductive systems. Required integrated laboratory includes frequent dissection.

title: Human Anatomy and Physiology II Lab
description: Offered autumn and spring. Offered at Missoula College. Coreq., BIOH 211N .  Restricted to students in the following majors: Missoula College Health Professions and Pre-Health Professions majors, Medical Information Technology, Pre-Physical Therapy, Athletic Training, and Health & Human Performance. Required companion laboratory for BIOH 211N . Basic knowledge necessary for students in health-related programs.  Emphasis on normal anatomy and physiology with presentation of basic concepts in chemistry and microbiology as they relate to human anatomy and physiology.  Covers endocrine through reproductive systems. A cadaver lab is included.

title: Special Topics
description: (R-6) Offered intermittently.  Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one-time offerings of current topics.

title: Independent Study
description: (R-6) Offered intermittently. Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student.

title: Student Teaching
description: (R-6) Offered intermittently. Offered at Missoula College. Organized student teaching.

title: Human Anatomy and Physiology for Health Professions I
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., CHMY 121N or CHMY 141N ; BIOB 160 or BIOH 112 or 113 . Introduction to basic cellular structure and function. The fundamental facts and concepts of the anatomy and physiology of cells and tissues, the integumentary, musculoskeletal, nervous and special senses with an emphasis on clinical application for students preparing for careers in health care.  Laboratory component includes presentation of cadaver prosections and models.

title: Human Anatomy and Physiology for Health Professions I Laboratory
description: Offered autumn. Prereq. or coreq., BIOH 365 . Laboratory-based instruction in the fundamental facts and concepts of the anatomy and physiology of cells and tissues, the integumentary, musculoskeletal, nervous and special senses with an emphasis on clinical application for students preparing for careers in health care. The laboratory uses cadavers and models.

title: Human Anatomy and Physiology for Health Professions II
description: Offered spring. Prereq., BIOH 365 . The fundamental facts and concepts of the anatomy and physiology of the endocrine, circulatory, respiratory, digestive, urinary and reproductive systems with an emphasis on clinical application for students preparing for careers in health care.

title: Human Anatomy and Physiology for Health Professions II Laboratory
description: Offered spring. Prereq. or coreq., BIOH 370 . Laboratory-based instruction in the fundamental facts and concepts of the anatomy and physiology of the endocrine, circulatory, respiratory, digestive, urinary and reproductive systems with an emphasis on clinical application for students preparing for careers in health care. The laboratory uses cadaver prosections and models.

title: Internship
description: (R-6) Offered every term. Extended classroom experience that provides practical application of learning during placement off campus. Prior approval must be obtained from the faculty supervisor. A maximum of 6 credits of Internship (198, 298, 398, 498) may count toward graduation.

title: Hematology
description: Offered spring, odd numbered years. Prereq., BIOH 365 or BIOH 201N . Requires junior standing. This course is intended to introduce the student to normal and pathologic hematology, with emphasis on blood cell development, cellular components, and normal vs. abnormal morphology. Clinical laboratory testing will be presented as a way to diagnose and monitor disease progression. Normal and pathologic coagulation will likewise be addressed. Treatment options for disease states will also be covered. Laboratory exercises will instruct the student in proper specimen collection, preparation of peripheral blood smears, microscopic examination of blood smears, and other manual tests associated with blood and coagulation studies. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Genes and Development Lab
description: Offered spring semester odd years. Prereq., BIOB 260 . BIOB 272 , BIOB 301 , and BIOB 375 are recommended. This is a laboratory course that will introduce the students to the tools and methods for investigating how genetic information drives animal development and to using model organisms for studying genes associated with human disease. Students will gain practical experience in experimental design, data collection, results analysis, and scientific communication. Students will perform research independently or in a small group as well as attend formal classroom presentations and discussion. Level: Undergraduate

title: Human Anatomy and Physiology I Tutor
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., BIOH 365 with a minimum grade of B. This select group of students performs tutoring for students enrolled in BIOH 365 and assists in preparation and grading of lecture and laboratory course teaching materials. Students enrolled in BIOH 461 have the option of co-enrolling in the cadaver dissection course. Level: Undergraduate

title: Principles of Medical Physiology
description: Offered intermittently. Prereq., WRIT 101 ; and BIOH 365 , 370 , and either CHMY 123 or 143N with a minimum grade of C. An advanced course in human physiology for students preparing for careers in health care. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Human Anatomy and Physiology II Tutor
description: Offered spring. Prereq., BIOH 370 with a minimum grade of B. This select group of students performs tutoring for students enrolled in BIOH 370 and assists in preparation and grading of lecture and laboratory course teaching materials. Students enrolled in BIOH 463 have the option of co-enrolling in the cadaver dissection course. Level: Undergraduate

title: Summer Clinical Laboratory
description: Offered summer. Students must have successfully completed the Medical Laboratory Science 3+1 on-campus curriculum and be admitted into an affiliated clinical practicum program. Professional training in clinical laboratory sciences for medical laboratory science. Level: Undergraduate

title: Professional Training I
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., BIOH 470 .  Continuation of BIOH 470 . Professional training at clinical site(s). Level: Undergraduate

title: Professional Training II
description: Offered spring. Prereq., BIOH 471 . Continuation of BIOH 471 . Professional training at clinical site(s). Level: Undergraduate

title: Teaching Anatomy & Physiology I
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., BIOH 365 and 370 with a minimum grade of B. This select group of students assists in preparation and grading of demonstrations and laboratory teaching materials and provides laboratory anatomy and physiology instruction to undergraduate students enrolled in BIOH 365 . Students enrolling for the 4 credit option will also provide occasional comparable assistance for BIOH 112 . Level: Undergraduate

title: Teaching Anatomy & Physiology II
description: Offered spring. Prereq., BIOH 365 and 370 with a minimum grade of B. This select group of students assists in the preparation and grading of demonstrations and laboratory teaching materials and provides laboratory anatomy and physiology instruction to undergraduate students. Students enrolling for the 4 credit option will also provide occasional comparable assistance for BIOH 113 . Level: Undergraduate

title: Special Topics
description: (R-10) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one-time offerings of current topics. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Microbiology for Health Sciences
description: Offered spring and summer. Infectious diseases, including concepts of virulence, resistance, prevention and control of microbial diseases in the individual and in the community. If laboratory experience is desired, the student may enroll concurrently in BIOM 251 . Credit not allowed toward a major in microbiology.

title: Microbiology Health Sciences Lab
description: Offered spring.  Prereq. or coreq., BIOM 250N . Observation of live microorganisms, their characteristics and activities. Experience with microbiological techniques. Credit not allowed toward a major in microbiology.

title: Special Topics
description: (R-6) Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one-time offerings of current topics.

title: Vector-Borne Diseases: Public Health Perspectives
description: Offered autumn. Restricted to students with at least junior standing. An exploration of major vector-borne diseases including Lyme disease, malaria, dengue fever, yellow fever, Zika, etc.  Lectures will cover disease history, biology and transmission dynamics, geographic and demographic trends, intervention strategies, and relationships to climate change.  Students will be encouraged to bring perspectives from any major field of study to discussions of the public health challenges of vector-borne diseases in the 21st century.

title: General Microbiology
description: Offered autumn and summer. Prereq., CHMY 123 or 143N . Prereq. or coreq., BIOB 260 . Microbial structure and function, growth and reproduction, physiology, ecology, genetics, environmental factors, control of microorganisms and sterility, antimicrobial agents, microbial diversity.

title: General Microbiology Lab
description: Offered autumn. Prereq. or coreq., BIOM 360 . Basic microbiology procedures and techniques.

title: Undergraduate Research
description: (R-12) Offered every term. Independent research under the direction of a faculty member. Graded credit/no credit.

title: Pathogenic Microbes
description: Offered spring.  Prereq., BIOM 360 .  A study of the pathogenic bacteria and fungi and the diseases they produce. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Pathogenic Microbes Laboratory
description: Offered spring. Prereq., BIOM 361 . Prereq. or coreq., BIOM 402 . Laboratory study of pathogenic bacteria and fungi. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Clinical Diagnosis
description: Offered spring. Prereq., BIOM 360 & BIOM 361 ; or BIOH 365 & BIOH 366 ; or BIOM 402 & BIOM 403 (may concur). Principles of blood chemistry, urinalysis, blood banking, serology and other clinical parameters of disease and health. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Clinical Diagnosis Lab
description: Offered spring. Prereq. or coreq., BIOM 407 . Clinical diagnostic methods. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Microbial Genetics
description: Offered spring even-numbered years. Prereq., BIOM 360 and BIOM 361 . The molecular genetics of prokaryotic organisms including: structure and replication of the prokaryotic chromosome; gene expression; mutagenesis and DNA repair; plasmids and other tools of genetic engineering; transmission of genetic material and recombination in prokaryotes; regulation of gene expression in prokaryotes; recombinant DNA and biotechnology. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Experimental Microbial Genetics Lab
description: Offered spring even-numbered years. Prereq. or coreq., BIOM 410 . Experiments in microbial genetics: Analysis of genes and genomes. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Microbial Diversity Ecology & Evolution
description: Offered spring. Prereq., BIOB 260 . BIOB 272 and BIOM 360 are recommended. A broad overview of the physiological, phylogenetic and genomic diversity and ecology of microorganisms within a framework of general ecological principles.  Focuses on microbial interactions with their environment at the level of the individual, population and community, including intimate associations with plants and animals.  Surveys current methods for studying microbial ecology and diversity in the environment. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Host-Microbe Interactions
description: Offered intermittently.  Prereq., BIOB 260 and BIOB 272 . Many organisms on the planet are hosts that interact with a diversity of microbes. Understanding these interactions is crucial to explain patterns of biodiversity and to improve the quality of human life. This course explores the diversity of host-microbe interactions in nature using the primary scientific literature as our guide. Level: Undergraduate

title: General Parasitology
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., BIOB 272 . Parasitism as a biological phenomenon, origin of parasitism, adaptations and life cycles, parasite morphology, fine structure, physiology, parasites and their environment. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: General Parasitology Lab
description: Offered autumn. Coreq., BIOM 427 . Taxonomy, morphology and identification of parasitic protozoa, helminths and arthropods. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Virology
description: Offered spring. Prereq., BIOB 260 . The general nature of viruses, with emphasis on the molecular biology of animal and human viruses. Co-convenes with BIOM 535 . Level: Undergraduate

title: Microbial Physiology
description: Offered autumn, odd-numbered years. Prereq., BIOM 360 . Microbial structure and function, physiological diversity, microbial metabolism, role of microbial activity in the environment. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Microbial Physiology Lab
description: Offered autumn, odd-numbered years. Prereq., BIOM 361 . Prereq. or coreq., BIOM 450 . Experimental approaches to analysis of microbial structure, composition and metabolism. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Ecology of Infectious Diseases
description: Offered  spring. In this course, we will take an ecological approach to understand infectious diseases. We will examine how diseases spread through time and space, and examine mathematical models of disease spread and their usefulness in control strategies. We will discuss case studies of both human and animal diseases, and the ecological concepts that apply to a wide range of systems. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Advanced Undergraduate Research
description: (R-10) Offered every term. Prereq., BIOM 360 . Restricted to students with at least junior standing. Independent research under the direction of a faculty member. Graded credit/no credit. Level: Undergraduate

title: Special Topics
description: (R-10) Offered intermittently.  Experimental offerings of new courses, experimental offerings of visiting professors, or one-time offerings of current topics. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Seminar
description: (R-3) Offered intermittently. Restricted to students with senior standing. Recent topics in microbiology and related subjects. Level: Undergraduate

title: Internship
description: (R-6) Offered every term. Extended classroom experience which provides practical application of classroom learning during placements off campus. Level: Undergraduate

title: Undergraduate Thesis
description: (R-6) Offered every term. Restricted to students with senior standing. Preparation of a thesis or manuscript based on undergraduate research for presentation and/or publication. Student must give an oral or poster presentation at the Biological Sciences Undergraduate Research Symposium or a scientific meeting. Level: Undergraduate

title: Advanced Immunology
description: Offered autumn even-numbered years.  Advanced topics and immunological techniques used in modern immunology. Level: Graduate

title: Advanced Virology
description: Coreq., BIOB 592 . A “principles-based” discussion of virology, focusing on the molecular processes and events that must be completed by all viruses for successful replication within an individual host, and spread through host populations.  The molecular basis of alternative replication strategies, the interactions of viruses with hosts organisms, and how these interactions lead to disease will be presented with examples drawn from a representative set of more well-understood animal viruses. BIOM 535 emphasizes independent, creative, critical thought. Co-convenes with BIOM 435 . Level: Graduate

title: Microbial Pathogenesis
description: Offered fall. Current concepts in pathogenesis at the molecular and cellular levels. Focus is on microbial (virla, bacterial) and genetic factors leading to disease and the host's involvement in the process. Level: Graduate

title: Experimental Microbial Ecology
description: Offered every term.  Focus on experimental design, methods, and presentation of experimental results in the area of microbial ecology. Level: Graduate

title: Intro to Research
description: (R-2) Offered autumn and spring.  Required course for biochemistry and microbiology graduate students. Instruction in basic research techniques, research equipment and reading in the relevant scientific literature. Students conduct research projects under faculty mentors of their choosing. Level: Graduate

title: Research
description: (R-40) Offered intermittently. Directed individual research and study appropriate to the background and objectives of the student. Level: Graduate

title: Special Topics
description: (R-24) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one-time offerings of current topics. Level: Graduate

title: Independent Study
description: (R-6) Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student. Level: Graduate

title: Molecular and Biomedical Sciences Seminar
description: (R-14) Presentation of current research in biochemistry and molecular biology by senior graduate students, faculty, and invited outside speakers. Level: Graduate

title: Thesis
description: (R-10) Offered intermittently. Laboratory research for and preparation of a master's thesis. Level: Graduate

title: Dissertation
description: (R-20) Offered intermittently. Laboratory research for and preparation of a doctoral dissertation. Level: Graduate

title: Introduction to Botany
description: Offered spring. Introduction to the plant kingdom including anatomy, physiology and ecology.

title: General Botany
description: Offered intermittently. Prereq., BIOB 260 and BIOB 272 .  Anatomy, morphology, ecology and physiology of photosynthetic organisms.

title: Rocky Mountain Flora
description: Offered spring. A prior college-level course in biology is recommended. Systematics of vascular plants with primary focus on flowering plant diversity and regionally important taxonomic groups. This integrated lecture and laboratory emphasizes applying general botanical terminology, characterizing major plant groups to recognize them without keying and understanding their functions (ecology) and origins (evolution), and the use of dichotomous keys for plant identification down the species level.

title: Biology and Management of Fishes
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., BIOB 272 and either STAT 216 or WILD 240 . Diversity, adaptations and ecology of fishes. Analysis and management of fish populations and communities.

title: Field Work/Clinical/Practicum
description: (R-12) Field work, clinical, or practicum experience in the biological sciences.

title: Plant Physiology
description: Offered intermittently.  Prereq., BIOB 260 . The molecular, biochemical and biophysical basis of plant function, from the subcellular to the whole organism level. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Plant Physiology Lab
description: Offered intermittently. Prereq. or coreq., BIOO 433 . Laboratory exercises designed to familiarize students with concepts and techniques in plant physiology. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Entomology
description: Offered intermittently. Prereq. or coreq., BIOB 272 . The classification, morphology, anatomy, development, life-history, behavior and ecology of insects.  Labs include identification of major insect groups, internal and external anatomy and student collections. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Ornithology
description: Offered spring. Prereq., or corequisite BIOB 272 . The classification, structure, evolution, behavior and ecology of birds. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Mammalogy
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., BIOB 272 . The evolution, systematics, anatomy, physiology and ecology of mammals. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Advanced Undergrad Research
description: (R-10) Offered every term. Restricted to students of at least junior standing. Independent research under the direction of a faculty member. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Ecosystem Ecology
description: Offered autumn every other year. This course includes the fundamentals of an ecosystem approach to ecological research by emphasizing relationships among physical, chemical, and biotic elements of interactive systems.  It will provide a fundamental basis for more advanced Systems Ecology courses (e.g., Limnology, Integrated Systems Ecology, Landscape Genetics, etc.). Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Integrated Systems Ecology
description: Offered spring semester alternate years.  Principles, theories and empirical studies that describe the complex attributes and processes of coupled natural and human systems.  Landscape, climate, economic and social change dynamics and processes emphasized. Flagship course of the UM-DBS Systems Ecology Program.  Students strongly advised but not required to take BIOS 532 Fundamentals of Ecosystem Ecology prior to this course. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Research
description: (R-15) Directed individual research and study appropriate to the background and objectives of the student. Level: Graduate

title: Special Topics
description: (R-24) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one-time offerings of current topics. Level: Graduate

title: Seminar
description: Offered intermittently. Presentations by student, faculty, and associates on issues and topics in their field. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Thesis
description: (R-15) Field and laboratory research on, and writing of, a student's masters thesis. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Dissertation
description: (R-20) Field and laboratory research on and writing of a student's doctoral dissertation. Level: Graduate

title: Special Topics
description: (R-6) Offered autumn and spring. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one time offerings of current topics.

title: Research Lab Rotations in Pharmaceutical Sciences
description: (R-6) Offered autumn and spring. Experience in research methods in departmental research laboratories. Level: Graduate

title: Research Seminar in Pharmaceutical Science
description: (R-9) Offered autumn and spring. Oral and written presentations of experimental research results and selected literature topics in the pharmaceutical sciences. Level: Graduate

title: Research Seminar in Toxicology
description: (R-9) Offered autumn and spring.  Oral and written presentations of experimental research results and selected literature topics in toxicology. Level: Graduate

title: Research (MS)
description: (R-10) Offered every term. Directed individual research and study appropriate to the background and objectives of the student. Level: Graduate

title: Special Topics
description: (R-24) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one time offerings of current topics. Level: Graduate

title: Independent Study
description: (R-9) Offered every term. Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student. Level: Graduate

title: Seminar
description: (R 6) Offered autumn and spring. Readings and discussion of current research literature. Level: Graduate

title: Thesis/Professional Paper (MS)
description: (R-10) Offered every term. Level: Graduate

title: Biomedical Research Ethics
description: Offered spring. Overview of biomedical research ethics and regulations. Topics include ethics and morality in science, scientific integrity, conflicts of interest, human and animal experimentation, intellectual property, plagiarism. Level: Graduate

title: Biomedical Statistics
description: Offered autumn.  Experimental design and statistical analysis relevant to the biomedical sciences. Level: Graduate

title: Pharmacology I
description: Offered autumn. Fundamentals of pharmacology and drug action. Level: Graduate

title: Pharmacology II
description: Offered spring. Prereq., BMED 613 . Fundamentals of pharmacology and drug action. Continuation of BMED 613 . Level: Graduate

title: Molecular Pharmacology
description: Offered alternate years. Focus on the basic theories, principles, and practical implications of receptor pharmacology to quantify drug activity. Major emphasis in pharmacodynamics with some time devoted to related pharmacokinetic parameters. Level: Graduate

title: Cardiovascular Pharmacology & Toxicology
description: Offered alternate years. Recent advances in pharmacology and toxicology of the cardiovascular system. In-depth study of regulatory mechanisms and the effect of immune response and xenobiotics on cardiovascular function. Level: Graduate

title: Drug Design: Medicinal Chemistry and Pharmacology
description: Offered in fall, alternate years. Students should have prior coursework in organic chemistry and biochemistry. Introduction to the main concepts in drug design and development including concepts and methods in medicinal chemistry and molecular pharmacology. Also covers computational methods for idea screening and lead development and the use of structural information in drug design and provides an overview of new chemical entity preclinical and clinical process. Level: Graduate

title: Drug Pharmacodynamics
description: Offered alternate years.  Organic Chemistry and Biochemistry or consent of instr.  Introduction and topical coverage of how drugs form complexes with biological targets to cause an array of responses. Level: Graduate

title: Drug Diversity
description: Offered alternate years.  Organic Chemistry and Biochemistry or consent of instr.  Topics in chemogenomics and diversity oriented synthesis will be covered. Level: Graduate

title: Methods in Medicinal Chemistry
description: Offered intermittently. Novel approaches to small molecule therapeutics for disease targeting. Level: Graduate

title: Drug Synthesis
description: Offered intermittently. An introduction to the past and current synthetic approaches and total syntheses of biologically active drugs. Level: Graduate

title: Research Methods in Biochemical Pharmacology
description: (R-6) Offered every term. Laboratory course intended to familiarize students with the instruments, and expertise of current research techniques in the biomedical sciences. Level: Graduate

title: Professional Development
description: Offered autumn and spring. Developmental training in presentations, writing, reviewing, literature research, teaching, research methods, grant writing, ethics, and business aspects in medicinal chemistry. Level: Graduate

title: Grantsmanship
description: This course is designed to provide graduate students and postdoctoral fellows with the necessary background, tools and hands on experience to be able to confidently write and submit a research grant.  The focus is on preparing a fellowship application although training will be provided for more typical investigator initiated grants.  The entire process from conception, preparation, review and revision will be covered.  This course will be a requirement for students on training grants.  No prerequisites are required. Level: Graduate

title: Pharmacogenetics
description: Offered alternate years. The genetic basis of differential drug activity. Level: Graduate

title: Drug Development: Pharmacokinetics
description: Offered fall. Recent developments and emerging concepts in theoretical and experimental pharmacokinetics, pharmacogenomics, and drug disposition.  Critical analysis of the current literature. Level: Graduate

title: Topics in Pharmaceutical Sciences and Drug Design
description: (R-12) Offered autumn and spring. Current topics in the pharmaceutical sciences, including pharmacology, pharmacokinetics, medicinal chemistry, and drug design and development. Level: Graduate

title: Toxicology I-Principles
description: Offered autumn. Introduction to toxicology. Topics include general principles, risk assessment, organ system toxicology, introduction to carcinogenesis, and genetic toxicology. Level: Graduate

title: Toxicology II-Agents
description: Offered spring. Prereq., BMED 641 . Toxic agents and the diseases caused by those agents. Includes common toxicants in the environment and occupational settings as well as drug induced toxicity. Level: Graduate

title: Cellular & Molecular Toxicology
description: Offered autumn.  Prereq., BMED 641 .  Cellular and molecular mechanisms of toxicity.  Includes apoptosis, regulation of cell cycle, genetic toxicology, and signal transduction pathways in toxicity. Level: Graduate

title: Immunopharmacology and Immunotoxicology
description: Offered alternate years. The impacts of xenobiotic agents on the immune system. Level: Graduate

title: Respiratory Toxicology
description: Offered alternate years.  Prereq., BMED 641 .  The lung and associated immune systems and their response to inhaled immunogenic and toxicological agents. Level: Graduate

title: Topics in Toxicology
description: (R-9) Offered autumn or spring. Current topics in toxicology. Level: Graduate

title: Topics in Immunology
description: (R-9) Offered autumn or spring. Current topics in immunology. Level: Graduate
