title: Thesis
description: (R-20) Offered every term. Restricted to graduate students in Wildlife Biology. Preparation of thesis. Level: Graduate

title: Introduction to Forestry Field Skills
description: Offered autumn. This course is focused on developing introductory forestry field skills through experiential learning at the Colleges Lubrecht Experimental Forest. Classroom lecture and experiences that introduce students to orienteering, map reading, GPS, tree measurements, fire and fuels management, recreation, human dimensions, hydrology, wood products, and the careers possible with a Forestry degree.

title: Urban Forestry
description: Offered spring. An introduction to urban forestry principles and practices. Benefits of the urban forest. Topics covered include plant species selection, site design, site assessment, planting, watering, fertilization, insects and diseases, pruning and tree care, inventory of property values, and community forestry development.

title: Special Topics
description: (R-6) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one-time offerings of current topics.

title: Independent Study
description: (R-6) Offered every term. Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student.

title: Forest Biometrics
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., M 115 or M 121 or M 122 or M 151 or M 162 or M 171 or M 172 . Introduction to probability and statistical methods for forestry and environmental sciences covering natural resource applications of common probability distributions, data analysis, hypothesis testing, and regression.

title: Forest Mensuration
description: Offered spring. Prereq., M 121 and M 122 or M 151 or M 162 or M 171 or M 172 ; and either prereq. or coreq. FORS 201 or STAT 216 or SOCI 202 or WILD 240 . The theory and practice of timber inventory and growth projection, including field measurements, sampling procedures, statistical methods, inventory compilation, and stand growth simulation under specified management prescriptions. Stand growth under specified management prescriptions.

title: Fire Management & Environmental Change
description: Offered Spring. Introduction to wildland fire and its role as a transformative process in the environment. Topics include pyrogeography, fire behavior, fire ecology, fire policy, and fire management. Examines the role of fire in shaping ecological and social systems, with a focus on societal issues of natural resources, human health, land use, climate change, and economics.  Provides foundational understanding of first principles. Serves as a stepping off point for further study of fire.

title: Forest Insects & Diseases
description: Offered spring. Identification, ecology and management of insects and pathogens affecting western North American forest.

title: Dendrology
description: Offered autumn and spring. Suggested coreq., FORS 240. Methods and techniques for identifying the major families of North American trees, based on gross morphological and anatomical features. Building and use of identification keys. Gen Ed Attributes: Natural Science Lab Course (N)

title: Intro to GIS for Forest Mgt
description: Offered every term. This course is designed as a practical introduction to the use of Geographic Information Systems (GIS) for storing, retrieving, analyzing and displaying spatial data. It will also cover the history of cartography and the conventions of the modern map-making process. Students need to register for a required lab section. Credit cannot be earned for both FORS 250 and GPHY 284 .

title: Special Topics
description: (R-6) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors; new courses or one-time offerings of current topics.

title: Independent Study
description: (R-8) Offered every term. Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student.

title: Internship
description: Offered fall. Restricted to majors in Forestry. Extended classroom experience that provides practical application of classroom learning during placements off campus. A maximum of 6 credits of Internship (198, 298, 398, 498) may count toward graduation.

title: Field Methods in Forest Ecology
description: This course introduces students to all aspects of forest demography and forest community ecology in the field. Particular attention is given to the agents of woody plant mortality, including beetle gallery identification, pathogenic fungi, spatially explicit density-dependent mortality, fire, and the effects of landscape position.  Students learn how data are collected to maximize information used to answer scientific questions, including the relationships between accuracy, precision, uncertainty, and cost (in time and money). Students then collect tree demography data within the Yosemite Forest Dynamics Plot.  Students learn how to measure fuel loading at landscape scales according to federal standards.  In addition to specific measurements in one forest type (white fir/sugar pine), students visit and compare the other principal forest types of the Sierra Nevada and White Mountains (ponderosa pine, red fir, Jeffrey pine, lodgepole pine, whitebark pine, pinyon/juniper, and bristlecone pine).

title: Forest Environmental Economics
description: Offered spring. Prereq. M 121 and M 122 or M 151 or M 162 or M 171 or M 172 .  Economic techniques to support decision making about the allocation of scarce resources in relation to the management of forests for timber and other ecosystem services.

title: Forest Ecology
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., one of BIOO 105N , BIOB 170N , BIOE 172N , BIOB 160 , or BIOB 101N ; and either prereq. or coreq. one of ENSC 245N, FORS 241N , NRSM 211N , or GPHY111N. Examination of physical and biological factors affecting forest structure, composition, and function, including biodiversity and species interactions, succession, disturbance, and nutrient cycling. Introduces foundational ecological theory and terminology, illustrated with examples from local, regional, and global forest ecosystems.

title: Wildland Fuel Management
description: Offered spring. Prereq., FORS 230 . The fire ecology of some western vegetation types is discussed.  Elements of the principles of wildland fuel management are presented.  Prescribed fire use and mechanical manipulation are matched to historic ecosystem processes.  Smoke management considerations and health issues are also presented.

title: Fire Ecology
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., FORS 230 or FORS 330 or BIOE 370 or BIOE 342 or NRSM 462 . A detailed analysis of fire ecology in terrestrial ecosystems with a focus on the Rocky Mountains, including fire history, fire effects, landscape patterns, land use legacies, management implications, and current topics. Includes at least one required all-day field trip.

title: Forest Ecology Field Laboratory
description: Prereq., FORS 130 or FORS 202 and coreq., FORS 330 . Field course with overnight camping. Must be able to safely travel off trail on steep, forested terrain. Introduces field techniques for measuring forest ecosystem attributes and illustrates foundational forest ecology theory and concepts with field visits to local and regional forests.

title: Sustainable Forest Products
description: Offered spring. Survey of forest products, including manufacture, use, markets, and impact on forest management. Life cycle analysis of wood products is compared to common alternatives. Labs include visits to area forest product manufacturing facilities including an all-day field trip.

title: Timber Harvesting & Roads
description: Offered spring. An overview of harvesting system capabilities and selection for multiple resource objectives. Fundamentals of forest road management. Best management practices as they apply to forest operations in Montana and the western United States.

title: Wood Anatomy, Properties, & Identification
description: Offered spring. Prereq., BIOO 105N or FORS 241N . Lecture and laboratory investigation of the structure, identification, and physical and mechanical properties of the commercial tree species of North America.

title: Multiple Resource Silviculture
description: Offered spring. Prereq., FORS 330 or BIOE 370 . Credit not allowed for both FORS 347 and 349 . An introduction to the concepts and application of silvicultural techniques to forest ecosystems to meet multiple resource objectives.

title: Practice of Silviculture
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., FORS 202 and FORS 241N . Prereq. or coreq., FORS 330 . Practice of Silviculture is designed primarily for Forestry majors (open to others with appropriate prerequisites), and will consider the conceptual foundations  behind various silvicultural practices and techniques, as well as and their application in forest ecosystems to meet multiple resource objectives. The course will cover natural stand dynamics, stand assessment and site classification schemes, even- and uneven-aged silvicultural systems, thinning/stand density concepts, regeneration practices, stand diagnosis and prescription development, vegetative management strategies for diverse objectives, along with quantitative assessment and modeling of alternative prescriptions.

title: Forestry Apps of GIS
description: Offered spring. Prereq., FORS 250 or GPHY 284 . Introduction to the basic concepts and techniques of computerized spatial data management and analysis systems and application to natural resource management.

title: Env Remote Sensing
description: Offered spring. The theory and application of photo- and electro-optical remote sensing for mapping resources and developing information systems.

title: Special Topics
description: (R-12) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one-time offerings of current topics.

title: Independent Study
description: (R-10) Offered every term. Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student.

title: Internship
description: (R-6) Offered every term. Extended classroom experience that provides practical application of classroom learning during placements off campus. Prior approval must be obtained from the faculty supervisor. A maximum of 6 credits of Internship (198, 298, 398, 498) may count toward graduation.

title: Advanced Forest Roads
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., FORS 341 . The purpose of this course is to help students understand the principles and skills of forest road design and the concepts of forest transportation planning. The course will cover the basic topics of road location, design, construction, and maintenance and provide students with techniques to identify the combination of roads, facilities and transport systems which minimize costs and negative environmental impacts. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Advaced Timber Harvesting
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., FORS 341 . This course covers the fundamentals of logging feasibility and cost analyses of various timber harvesting systems including the characteristics and performance of ground vehicles, cable and aerial systems; cost factors and cost analysis procedures; safety issues; and environmental impacts of harvesting systems. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Forest Stand Management
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., FORS 202 and FORS 341 and FORS 347 or FORS 349 . The management and manipulation of forest stands to reach multiple objectives, with a focus on the planning of forest operations for a community partner. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Applied Methods in Forest Restoration and Utilization
description: (R-9) Offered every term. Meeting all day on Saturdays, and some Sundays, this course involves training students to safely and efficiently identify forest stands to be restored through appropriately-planned management activities including both live and dead timber felling operations, manufacture of sawlogs and pulpwood, proper management of slash and residuals, grapple skidding and the production of lumber using both circular sawmill and bandsaw mill. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Forest Planning
description: Offered spring. Prereq., FORS 320 ; and FORS 347 or FORS 349 . Integrated multiple use planning at the forest-wide level: defining multi-resource management goals, generating management alternatives, projecting outcomes, assessing environmental impacts, and implementing preferred option. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Special Topics
description: (R-12) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one-time offerings of current topics. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Independent Study
description: (R-10) Offered every term. Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student. Level: Undergraduate

title: Wildland RxFire Practicum
description: Students should have prior fire experience. An intensive field course providing students with technical training, practical applications, and theoretical foundations in ecological burning for restoration purposes. Class is typically held in southeastern United States. Co-convened with FORS 544 . Level: Undergraduate

title: Internship
description: Offered every term. Extended classroom experience that provides practical application of classroom learning during placements off campus. Prior approval must be obtained from the faculty supervisor. A maximum of 6 credits of Internship (198, 298, 398, 498) may count toward graduation. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Senior Thesis
description: Offered autumn and spring. Restricted to students with senior standing. Preparation of a major paper based on study or research in a field selected according to the needs and objectives of the student. Level: Undergraduate

title: Sampling Methods
description: Offered spring. Fundamentals of statistical sampling emphasizing natural and environmental resource applications.  Principles of inferences and alternative estimators are studied in the context of simple random, systematic, unequal probability, stratified, and 3P/Poisson designs.  Variable radius plot sampling, line intersect sampling, and other probability proportional to size designs used in forest and ecological inventories are also covered. Level: Graduate

title: Forest Community Ecology
description: Prior coursework or experience in ecology, botany, or forestry is strongly recommended. This course provides a foundation in ecological theory as well as an introduction to current findings and recent advances in forest community ecology, stand structure dynamics, and forest disturbance process. Level: Graduate

title: Applied Forest Ecology
description: This course covers the use of ecological theory and data in the design of silvicultural treatments to achieve multiple management objectives, with particular emphasis on forest restoration and climate change adaptation. We examine methods of silvicultural design, including use of historical and contemporary reference conditions, and climate adaptation strategies. Analysis exercises use the open source statistical program and language R for data analysis, visualization, and modeling, especially of spatial point pattern data. Introduction to monitoring and adaptive management of silvicultural treatments. Level: Graduate

title: Ecological Statistics
description: Offered autumn. This is an applied course covering advanced statistical modeling techniques using examples from forestry, ecology, and the environmental sciences. Covers data management, visualization, and scripting with R, an open source data analysis and statistics platform. Explores various parametric and semi-parametric modeling strategies that allow for non-linear response functions and/or non-Gaussian response distributions. Estimation and inference in the context of generalized linear models, generalized additive models, and classification and regression trees are discussed using examples from the scientific literature. Lays the foundation for subsequent graduate-level analytic coursework. Level: Graduate

title: Fire and Disturbance Ecology
description: This course covers foundational concepts in disturbance ecology and fire ecology, examines important and influential theories in both fields, and focuses on currently literature at the cutting-edge of fire ecology research. Level: Graduate

title: Adv. Wildland RXFire Practicum
description: An intensive field course providing students with technical training, practical applications, and theoretical foundations in ecological burning for restoration purposes. Students will practice leadership skills by supervising and training fire personnel in application of prescribed fire. Class typically held in southeastern United States. Co-convened with FORS 495 . Level: Graduate

title: Landscape Ecology
description: Offered Spring semester even years. Prereq., FORS 538 . The purpose of this course is to provide students with an introduction to the discipline of landscape ecology with a focus on applications within ecology and natural resource management. In addition to studying the fundamentals of landscape ecology through reading primary literature, students will gain exposure to a range of tools used in the analysis of geo-spatial data including raster analysis, environmental remote sensing, state transition models and species distribution models. Another objective is to engage students in student-directed learning within an inter-disciplinary environment to improve and refine students oral and written communication skills. Level: Graduate

title: Graduate Research
description: (R-15) Offered every term. Independent research under the direction of a faculty member. Level: Graduate

title: Special Topics
description: (R-12) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one-time offerings of current topics. Level: Graduate

title: Independent Study
description: (R-10) Offered every term. Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student. Level: Graduate

title: Graduate Seminar
description: (R-12) Offered autumn. A review and discussion of current research. Topics vary. Level: Graduate

title: Internship
description: (R-15) Offered every term. Extended classroom experience that provides practical application of classroom learning during placements off campus. Prior approval must be obtained from the faculty supervisor. Level: Graduate

title: Professional Paper
description: (R-15) Offered autumn and spring. Preparation of Master of Ecosystem Management professional paper. Level: Graduate

title: Graduate Research
description: (R-15) Offered every term. Independent graduate research in forest management, wood science, soils, wildlife management, silviculture, recreation and other topic areas. Level: Graduate

title: Thesis
description: (R-30) Offered every term. Preparation of thesis/dissertation. Level: Graduate

title: Elementary French I
description: Offered autumn. Active skills: listening, speaking, reading and writing plus basic cultural analysis.

title: Elementary French II
description: Prereq., FRCH 101 . Offered spring. Continuation of 101.

title: Special Topics
description: (R–9) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one–time offerings of current topics.

title: Intermediate French I
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., FRCH 102 . Expansion of active skills: listening, speaking, reading, writing plus further cultural analysis.

title: Intermediate French II
description: Offered spring. Prereq., FRCH 201 . Continuation of 201.

title: Special Topics
description: (R-9) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one-time offerings of current topics.

title: Independent Study
description: (R–6) Offered autumn and spring. Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student.

title: Seminar/Workshop
description: (R-6) Offered intermittently. A review and discussion of current research. Topics vary.

title: Advanced Grammar/Oral and Written Expression
description: Prereq., FRCH 202 . Advanced grammar review including literary tenses; developmental and written skills in French.

title: French Literature and Culture: Middle Ages and Renaissance
description: Offered intermittently. Prereq., FRCH 301 . French literature of the Middle Ages and Renaissance with a focus on cultural identity.

title: French Literature and Culture: 17th Century
description: Offered spring. Prereq., FRCH 202 or FRCH 301 . French literature of the 17th century within its cultural context.

title: French Literature and Culture: 19th Century
description: Offered spring. Prereq., FRCH 301 . French literature from the 19th Century within its cultural context.

title: French Literature and Culture: 20th Century
description: Offered spring. Prereq., FRCH 301 . Survey of literature and culture of 20th Century France, with a focus on the significance of cultural identity.

title: French Literature and Culture: 18th Century
description: Offered fall. Prereq., FRCH 301 . French literature of the 18th century within its cultural context.

title: Literature and Culture of the Francophone World
description: Prereq., FRCH 301 . Survey of literature and culture of the diverse Francophone World with a focus on the significance of plural cultural identities.

title: The French Cinema
description: (R–6) Offered intermittently. An historical, aesthetic, and critical survey of the French cinema, from its beginnings in 1895 through the contemporary cinema (Muet, classical, Realism, Nouvelle Vogue, etc.) With an introduction to contemporary film criticism.  Students taking the course for French credits are required to do research, reading, and writing in the French language.

title: Survey of African Cinema
description: Offered intermittently.  A diachronic survey of African cinema accompanied by interpretation and evaluation of textual dimensions of films through filmic critical theory.

title: French Civilization & Culture
description: (R-6) Offered spring. Prereq., FRCH 301 . Chronological/topical study of French culture.

title: Special Topics
description: (R–9) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one–time offerings of current topics.

title: Independent Study
description: (R–3) Offered autumn and spring. Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student.

title: Seminar/Workshop
description: (R-12) Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student.

title: Studies in French Prose
description: Offered intermittently. Prereq., FRCH 301 and one of FRCH 310 , FRCH 311 , FRCH 312 , or FRCH 313 . Evolution of textuality from the Renaissance to the 20th century: the novel. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Advanced Stylistics & Oral Arguments
description: Prereq., FRCH 301 and one of FRCH 310 , FRCH 311 , FRCH 312 or FRCH 313 . Intensive analysis of usage and style in written and oral argumentation at various linguistic levels. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Studies in French Drama
description: Offered intermittently. Prereq., FRCH 301 and one of FRCH 310 , FRCH 311 , FRCH 312 or FRCH 313 . Evolution of theatre from the Renaissance to the 20th century or performance of a French play in French. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Special Topics
description: (R-9) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one-time offerings of current topics. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Independent Study
description: (R-6) Offered autumn and spring. Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Seminar/Workshop
description: (R-9) Offered autumn and spring. Studies in major authors, periods, genres, and/or cultural studies. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Intro to Engineering Calculation & Problem Solving
description: Offered autumn. Prereq. or coreq., M 151 , or M 121 and M 122 , or M 171 or M 172 or ALEX score of 5, or M03-Maplesoft Calculus score >= 15. An introduction to engineering calculations, problem solving, and design. Students are taught to solve and present engineering problems on computers using spreadsheet and graphic software (AutoCAD). In addition, there will be discussions on engineering failures and engineering ethics.

title: Engineering Statics
description: Prereq., PHSX 215N and M 171 . Prereq. or coreq., M 172 . Equilibrium of particles and rigid bodies; addition and resolution of forces, vector algebra, moments and couples, resultants and static equilibrium, equivalent force systems, centroids, center of gravity, free body method of analysis, two and three dimensional equilibrium, trusses, frames, friction, and method of virtual work.

title: Engineering Mechanics - Dynamics
description: Prereq., EGEN 201 and M 172 . Particle and rigid body kinematics and kinetics; rectilinear, curvilinear, and relative motion, equations of motion, work and energy, impulse and momentum, systems of particles, rotation, rotating axes, rigid body analysis, angular momentum, vibration, and time response.

title: Special Topics
description: (R-24) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one-time offerings of current topics.

title: Intro to Physical Geography: Climate, Landforms, and Vegetation
description: Offered spring. Introduction to earth system science, the study of the earth’s climate, surface processes, and ecosystems, and their distributions, functions, and interactions.

title: Intro to Physical Geography Laboratory: Climate, Landforms, and Vegetation Lab
description: Offered spring. Prereq. or coreq., GPHY 111N . Introduction to techniques and methods needed to understand and analyze data, graphs, maps, and images of the earth’s climate, surface processes, and ecosystems, and their interactions.

title: Human Geography
description: Offered autumn and spring. Introduction to Human Geography focuses upon the linkages between geography and society including analysis of regions, ethnic groups, urban landscapes, migration and population change, geopolitics, economics, and cultural differences.

title: Geography of World Regions
description: Offered autumn and/or spring. An overall view of how the lands and peoples of the world are organized into coherent geographical regions, how landscapes differ from region to region, and how the people differ in terms of their traits, beliefs, ways of life, and economic livelihood.

title: Montana
description: Offered autumn.  The physical, cultural, economic, political,  and historical geography of the state including Montana’s mountains and the prairies.

title: The Middle East
description: Offered intermittently. A survey of the biophysical and cultural geography of Southwest Asia and North Africa. Emphasis on environmental change; prehistory; patterns of cultural and historical change; issues of socio-economic, religious, and political diversity; and the broader political significance of the region.

title: Intro to GIS and Cartography
description: Offered every term. This course is designed as a practical introduction to the use of Geographic Information Systems (GIS) for storing, retrieving, analyzing and displaying spatial data. It will also cover the history of cartography and the conventions of the modern map-making process. Students need to register for a required lab section. Credit cannot be earned for both FORS 250 and GPHY 284 .

title: Special Topics
description: (R-6) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one-time offerings of current topics.

title: Mountain Field Studies
description: (R-6) Offered autumn and spring as a series of one-credit courses with a maximum of three-credits per semester. Field studies of Montana's Rocky Mountain Front, Crown of the Continent, or Yellowstone. Students prepare to conduct field work, spend time in the field observing wildlife, physical landscapes and cultural aspects of these landscapes, and follow up their observations in written reports.

title: Biogeography
description: Offered intermittently. Changing patterns of plant and animal distributions in space and time. Combination of historical and ecological approaches to biological species and communities. Study of external causes of plant and animal distributions, especially climatic change and human impacts.

title: Geomorphology
description: Offered autumn even-numbered years. Prereq., GPHY 111N or GEO 101N . Important landforms and landscapes, their biophysical processes, and their formative elements.

title: Economic Geography of Rural Areas
description: Offered spring odd-numbered years. Study of the location of economic activities, including agriculture, industry, and services.  Focus on the changing nature of rural areas.

title: Water and Sustainability
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., WRIT 101 or WRIT 201 . Geographical perspective on water resource issues and challenges facing the public, resource managers, and water users in the western United States. Examines concepts, terms, and regulatory environment which provide the foundation for sustainable water management and policy.

title: Mountains and Society
description: Offered spring. Physical and cultural aspects of the mountains of North and South America, Europe, Africa, and Asia. Emphasis on combining the physical landscape with an overview of the indigenous people who inhabit the worlds’ heights.

title: Field Studies in Geography
description: (R-12)  Offered autumn and spring.  Through extended backcountry travel, experiential examination of regional landforms, climate, hydrology, soils, and patterns of vegetation and wildlife.  Local landscapes, natural-resource endowment, and societies with particular emphasis on human-environmental interaction.  Geographical skills and techniques, including map reading and navigational skills.  Offered by the Wild Rockies Field Institute as part of a semester-long, 12-credit field experience with corequisite courses in allied fields.

title: Preceptorship in Geography
description: (R-6) Offered autumn and spring. Restricted to students with at least junior standing. Assisting a faculty member by tutoring, conducting review sessions, helping students with research projects, and carrying out other class-related responsibilities.

title: Field Techniques
description: Offered autumn and intermittently in spring. Field techniques used by geographers and planners in making field observations and in collecting data.

title: Special Topics
description: (R-12) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one-time offerings of current topics.

title: Independent Study
description: (R-9) Offered every term. Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student.

title: Seminar
description: (R-9) Offered intermittently. A review and discussion of current research. Topics vary.

title: Field Studies
description: (R-9) Offered fall and/or spring. Field experience in the region. Includes geographically relevant field courses offered as part of Swan Valley Connections' Landscape and Livelihood Field Semester, focusing on natural and human communities and on conservation solutions of the Southwest Crown of the Continent Region: Sustainability and Agriculture (3 cr), and Biogeography of Northwest Montana (4 cr).

title: Geography Capstone
description: Offered autumn. Restricted to students with senior standing. Exploration of current research, projects, and programs of geographers and scientists/practitioners in allied disciplines and fields, and preparation of a professional portfolio. Student preparation for post-graduate professional and academic careers is emphasized. Level: Undergraduate

title: Sustainable Cities
description: Offered spring even-numbered years. Restricted to students with at least junior standing. A discussion of sustainability efforts in cities around the world. Topics include, for example, urban sprawl and smart growth, alternative energy, public transportation, integrated waste management, integrated water management, green architecture, and urban agriculture. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Community Resilience
description: Prereq., WRIT 101 or WRIT 201 . Examines human-environment relations and interactions. Topics include: human geographic perspectives on landscape, cultural ecology, political ecology, community resilience in relation to complex adaptive systems, and planning applications. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Planning Principles & Processes
description: Offered autumn even-numbered years. Restricted to students with at least junior standing. Surveys planning principles, practices and issues in urban and rural environments. Attention is devoted to Montana, state planning programs in the United States., and federal programs and policies that influence land-use planning. Emphasizes skills and techniques used in plan development and implementation. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Environmental Planning
description: Offered autumn odd-numbered years. Introduction to practice of environmental planning which includes elements of physical planning, planning design at the landscape scale, and conservation planning. Includes field visits and project-based work. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Community & Regional Analysis
description: Offered autumn. Coreq., GPHY 469 . Socio-demographic analysis of communities and regions: population, employment, and spatial interaction. Hands-on course designed for future planners, GIS analysts, and others interested in socio-demographic change. To succeed in this course students should have comfort with basic algebra. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Planning & Analysis Laboratory
description: Offered autumn.  Coreq., GPHY 468 .  Laboratory to accompany GPHY 468 . Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: UAV Remote Sensing for Field Ecology
description: Offered summer only at the Flathead Lake Biological Station. Prereq., FORS 250 or GPHY 284 . This course will introduce students to field-based methods of close-range remote sensing in freshwater ecosystems. Students will gain knowledge of basic spatial analysis through GIS and remote sensing techniques. Students will learn basic applications of UAVs and Acoustic Doppler Profilers, two remote sensing instruments of fast-growing interest in ecological research and application. Students will learn about essentials to operate UAVs and ADPs, initial post-processing of data products and integrating these data into ecological research and application. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Advanced Cartographic Design
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., GPHY 284 or FORS 250 . This course concentrates on the presentation of spatial data and the construction of cartographic products that have clear communication and excellent aesthetic design. The class meets the University's service learning course objectives through a semester long project where students consult with a client, design and construct a map, and deliver a final product. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Spatial Analysis & GIS
description: Offered intermittently. Prereq., GPHY 284 or FORS 250 . Coreq., GPHY 489 . Quantitative analysis of spatial data, including techniques for pattern analysis, classification, and interpolation within a GIS environment. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Internet GIS
description: Offered intermittently. Prereq., GPHY 284 or FORS 250 . Principles and techniques for distributing GIS and mapping applications through the Internet. Students need to register for a required linked lab section. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Transport, Planning & GIS
description: Coreq., GPHY 489 . A project-oriented course focusing on patterns and trends in urban passenger transportation, principles of transport planning, and modeling in GIS-T. To succeed in this course students should have comfort with basic algebra and statistics. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Remote Sensing/Raster GIS
description: Offered autumn. Prereq. or coreq., GPHY 284 or FORS 250 . Coreq., GPHY 489 . Basic principles of remote sensing and analyzing images within a raster GIS. Review current data sources. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Applications of GIS
description: Offered spring. Prereq., GPHY 284 or FORS 250 . Application of GIS for managing natural and cultural resources. Covers choropleth maps, dot maps, proportional figure maps, isarithmic maps, and others. Includes computer mapping and GIS exercises. Students need to register for a required linked lab section. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Cartography/GIS Laboratory
description: (R-4) Offered autumn and spring. Prereq., or coreq., GPHY 482 , 486 or 487 . Lab to accompany cartography and GIS courses. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Special Topics
description: (R-9) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one-time offerings of current topics. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Independent Study
description: (R-9) Offered every term. Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Workshop in Teaching Geography
description: Offered intermittently. Concepts and techniques in geography, with emphasis on their use in teaching geography in Montana schools. Students are required to prepare and present a teaching unit project. Designed for pre-service or in-service teachers. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Internship
description: Offered every term. Extended classroom experience that provides practical application of classroom learning during placements off campus. Prior approval must be obtained from the faculty supervisor. A maximum of 6 credits of Internship (198, 298, 398, 498) may count toward graduation.

title: Senior Thesis
description: (R-6) Offered autumn and spring. Prereq., WRIT 101 . Restricted to students with senior standing. Independent research project in any geographical topic supervised by a faculty member and leading to completion of the baccalaureate degree. Level: Undergraduate

title: Geography Graduate Colloquium
description: (R-3) Presentations of faculty and student research and guest lecturers. Graded credit/no-credit only. Level: Graduate

title: Research Design in the Geographical Sciences I
description: Understanding of diverse research approaches in the geographical sciences and development of a thesis project. Level: Graduate

title: Research Design in Geographical Sciences II
description: Prereq., or corequisite GPHY 504 . Preparation of a thesis proposal: research design, data collection, analysis, interpretation, and presentation. Level: Graduate

title: Seminar in Paleoclimate
description: Offered every other autumn.  Covers the major controlling factors of climate through time, the variety of paleoclimate reconstruction methods, the range of impacts of climate on previous civilizations, and provides context for our current climate situation. Level: Graduate

title: Seminar in Geography
description: (R-9) Offered intermittently. Seminar topics in geography and society, human-environmental interaction, physical geography, regional geography, or geographical techniques. Level: Graduate

title: Seminar in Planning
description: Offered spring odd-numbered years.  A critical analysis of land planning history, theory, approaches, and practice.  Emphasis is on the United States and England. Level: Graduate

title: Planning Design
description: Analysis of land-use problems and design. Level: Graduate

title: Preceptorship in Geography
description: (R-6) Assisting a faculty member by tutoring, helping students with research projects, and carrying out other class-related activities. Level: Graduate

title: Seminar GIS & Cartography
description: (R-9) Offered every two years. Seminar topics in cartography and GIS. Applications to advanced studies in human and physical geography. Level: Graduate

title: Image Analysis & Modeling
description: Offered every two years. Prereq., GPHY 487 or FORS 351 . Coreq., GPHY 589 .  Advanced topics in image analysis (e.g. hyperspectral images and pattern-recognition-based classification) and foundations of simple raster-based models. Level: Graduate

title: Cartography/GIS Laboratory
description: (R-4) Laboratory to accompany GPHY 587 . Level: Graduate

title: Special Topics
description: (R-24) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one-time offerings of current topics. Level: Graduate

title: Introduction to Physical Geology
description: Offered autumn and spring. Broad introduction to physical earth systems including the work of wind, flowing water, glacial ice, gravity, earthquakes, volcanoes and plate tectonics in shaping the earth's environment.

title: Introduction to Physical Geology Lab
description: Offered autumn and spring. Prereq. or coreq., GEO 101N . A series of laboratory and field experiences designed around basic geologic processes and materials.  Familiarization with common minerals, rocks, land forms, and structures.

title: Oceanography
description: Offered spring. The ocean covers 70 % of the globe, and yet vast regions remain unexplored. Interactions between the atmosphere and the sea moderate and control our climate. Nearly 40 % of the world’s population lives within 100 kilometers of the coast. The oceans are geographically, environmentally, culturally, and economically critical to society. This course introduces oceanography, including the origin of water and ocean basins; marine resources; atmospheric circulation; air-sea interaction; ocean-climate feedback; currents, tides, and coastal processes; marine ecology; and use and misuse of the oceans.

title: Natural Disasters
description: Offered intermittently. This course introduces the scientific context and latest research on natural hazards and disasters, including storms, flood, drought, mass wasting (landslides and avalanches), earthquakes and tsunamis, volcanic eruptions, and wildfires.

title: Special Topics
description: (R–6) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one–time offerings of current topics.

title: Field Geosciences Methods
description: (R-6) Field-based instruction of basic geosciences principles. Geosciences field trips around Missoula and classroom meetings to integrate field observations.

title: Internship
description: (R-6) Offered every term. Extended classroom experience that provides practical application of classroom learning during placements off campus. Prior approval must be obtained from the faculty supervisor. A maximum of 6 credits of Internship may count toward graduation.

title: The Rocky Planet
description: Offered autumn and spring. Prereq., ERTH 101N or GEO 101N or GEO 105N or GEO 107N . Presents scientific evidence and interpretations as to Earth's evolution and history as preserved in the global rock record, including the development of the oceans, atmosphere, climate and biota. Traces Earth's geologic history through time.

title: The Water Planet
description: Offered spring. Prereq., ERTH 101N or GEO 101N or GEO 105N or GEO 107N ; and GEO 102N . This course will introduce students to the study of the hydrologic cycle and associated earth-surface processes. The course will describe the mechanisms that control the movement of water and the influence of water fluxes on landscapes, ecosystems, and humans.

title: General Science: Physics & Geoscience
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., M 095 or ALEKS placement >= 4 or M02-Maplesoft Algebra score >= 12. Integrated lectures, discussions, laboratory exercises, and demonstrations on topics in chemical and physical science for prospective elementary school teachers and the non-scientist. A two-hour laboratory session is required each week.

title: Special Topics
description: (R-6) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one–time offerings of current topics.

title: Seminar
description: (R-10) Offered intermittently. Independent study of various topics under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Mineralogy and Optical Mineralogy
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., GEO 201 and either CHMY 121N or CHMY 141N . Identification, properties, occurrence, and associations of the rock-forming minerals; introduction to crystallography (crystal classes, lattice types, and external morphology) and crystal chemistry (bonding and crystal structure types); analytical techniques including mineral optics, x-ray, and SEM analysis. $50.00 field trip and lab fee.

title: Igneous & Metamorph Petrology
description: Offered spring. Prereq., GEO 302 . Characteristics, phase relations, and origin of igneous and metamorphic rocks. Classification and interpretation of igneous and metamorphic rocks in hand specimen and thin section. Plate tectonic settings of magmatism and metamorphism.

title: Sedimentation/Stratigraphy
description: Offered spring. Prereq., GEO 201 and 302 . Origins of sediments and sedimentary rocks; climate, weathering, and weathering products; transport, deposition, and depositional environments of sediments; concepts and methods of stratigraphy including correlation of sedimentary rocks and an introduction to basin analysis.

title: Getting Started in Research
description: This course introduces students to the process of research and creative inquiry, including how to formulate a research question, how to navigate academic literature, how to develop a research plan, how to connect with faculty mentors, and how to discover opportunities for internships and graduate school. The course prepares students to participate and thrive in undergraduate research experiences, such as a senior thesis, capstone project, or internship. Students may be new to research or already involved in research.

title: Earth's Changing Climate
description: Offered autumn and/or summer. Restricted to students with at least junior standing. The future of our climate from the perspective of climate system dynamics. The course has three five-week modules. The first covers the processes and time scales governing Earth’s heat balance, the basic rules of changing climate. The second module explores climate variability stemming from internal dynamics of the climate system. The final module examines the grand challenge in climate science: constraining projections of future change with regards to feedbacks and uncertainties in climate system processes. Emerging research is highlighted throughout the course on unsettled scientific problems in climate science and the approaches taken by scientists to solve them.

title: Surface Processes
description: Offered spring. Prereq., GEO 101N / GEO 102N or ERTH 101N / ERTH 103N ; and M 122 or M 151 . Study of the earth's surface and near surface using the laws and principles of physics. Mechanisms underlying the processes that shape the earth's surface and drive its evolution, including climate, hydrology, geomorphology, and geochemistry.

title: Earth Resources and Sustainability
description: Prereq., ERTH 101N , GEO 101N , or GEO 107N . This course explores the nature and critical issues of major types of Earth resources - energy, metallic, non-metallic, water, soil - and the impacts that resource usage has on the Earth environment from both geologic and societal perspectives. Content will include timescales and geological processes of formation and distribution of resource materials and the scope for environmental and climatic change at the Earth's surface. The course will bring together the science of Earth resources with the broader human context of resource exploitation.

title: Energy and the Environment
description: This course examines all forms of energy used by humans historically and today including renewable and conventional forms. Each energy form will be examined in terms of the technology behind it, sustainability pros/cons particularly as regards the environment, and the potential role of each for stabilizing atmospheric carbon dioxide levels.

title: Computational Methods for Earth and Environmental Scientists
description: Prereq., M 121 . In this course, students obtain a panoramic and practical understanding of computational methods commonly used in the field of Earth and Environmental Sciences and learn how to use the Python programming language to apply these methods for environmental data analysis, The course covers topics in statistical inference, hypotheses testing, solution of linear and nonlinear system and solution of ordinary differential equations.

title: Research
description: Offered intermittently. Directed individual research and study appropriate to the back ground and objectives of the student. Level: Undergraduate

title: Special Topics
description: (R-9) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one–time offerings of current topics.

title: Independent Study
description: (R-6) Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student.

title: Seminar/Workshop
description: (R-9) Offered intermittently. A review and discussion of current research. Topics vary.

title: Internship
description: (R-6) Offered every term. Extended classroom experience that provides practical application of classroom learning during placements off campus. Prior approval must be obtained from the faculty supervisor. No more than 3 credits of GEO 398 may be applied to the geosciences minor. A maximum of 6 credits of Internship may count toward graduation.

title: Careers in Geosciences
description: Geoscience graduates have a rich portfolio of career options. This course explores the spectrum of career available to Geoscience students and the tools to ensure a successful career. Invited speakers, mostly alumni of the Geosciences program at UM, describe their career, the skills required by their profession, and provide advice on how to maximize education for success in their field. Speakers are chosen to represent the broad spectrum of career paths and employer opportunities in the Geosciences. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Hydrogeology
description: Offered spring. Prereq., GEO 101N / GEO 102N or ERTH 101N / ERTH 103N ; and PHSX 205N / PHSX 206N or PHSX 215N / PHSX 216N ; and M 171 . Occurrence, movement, quality, and methods of quantification of groundwater. Geological framework and physics of groundwater flow. Supply, contamination, and management problems. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Hydrology
description: Offered autumn. Introduction to the physical mechanisms that drive the water cycle at different scales. The course covers heat, momentum and mass transfer and storage mechanisms in turbulent systems and their role in the global and local climates. At the local scale, the equations that govern surface and subsurface water flows are studied. Along with the overarching goals, students will improve their quantitative skills, will gain experience accessing and reading the professional literature and will improve their capabilities to acquire knowledge independently. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Field Methods: Earth, Water, and Climate Science
description: In this class students will develop basic knowledge required to perform field work in remote locations across a broad range of disciplines in the earth, water, and climate sciences. Students will be introduced to a wide variety of field skills from backcountry navigation to understanding instrumentation and measurement principles, sources of uncertainty error, and data reduction. Students will make a variety of different measurements and learn to interpret their collected data. Level: Undergraduate

title: Geophysics
description: Prereq., GEO 101N / GEO 102N or ERTH 101N / ERTH 103N ; and PHSX 207N or PHSX 217N ; and M 171 . This course will explore a variety of fundamental topics in geophysics, including Earth formation, Earth structure, plate tectonics, gravity, seismology, heat flow and magnetics. The course will also cover modern geophysical surveying methods, including reflection seismology, refraction seismology, gravity surveying, and magnetic surveying. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Process Geomorphology
description: Prereq., GEO 202 or NRSM 210N; and M 121 or M 122 or M 151 . Quantitative examination of landforms, runoff generation, weathering, mechanics of soil erosion by water and wind, mass wasting, glacial  processes and hillslope evolution. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate.

title: Snow, Ice and Climate Change
description: Offered autumn. Restricted to students with at least junior standing. Frozen water (i.e., glaciers, ice sheets, seasonal snow, and sea ice) is a primary component of Earth's climate system that both drives and responds to climate change. This course examines the role of snow and ice in four pressing issues of climate change: 1) global sea level rise and stability of ice sheets; 2) Arctic amplification of climate change and sea ice processes; 3) climate system feedbacks revealed by ice cores; and, 4) water supply and mountain snowpack dynamics. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Special Topics
description: (R-8) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of  visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses or one-time offerings of current topics. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Independent Study
description: (R-6) Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Senior Geology Seminar
description: (R-10) Offered intermittently. Restricted to majors in Geosciences with at least junior standing. A review and discussion of current research. Topics vary. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Internship
description: (R-6) Offered every term. Extended classroom experience that provides practical application of classroom learning during placements off campus. Prior approval must be obtained from the faculty supervisor. A maximum of 6 credits of Internship (198, 298, 398, 498) may count toward graduation. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Senior Thesis/Capstone
description: (R-10) Offered every term. Prereq., WRIT 101 . Independent research project in any geosciences topic supervised by faculty member, and leading to completion of baccalaureate degree. Level: Undergraduate

title: Fundamentals of Academic Research
description: Offered autumn. An introduction to scientific research including strategies for designing projects, research methods, scientific tools, proposals and publishing, and the culture and trends within the field of geosciences. Intended for first semester graduate students in geosciences. Level: Graduate.

title: Research Workshop
description: This course includes group discussion, analysis, and troubleshooting of research challenges. Analogous to writers' and artists' workshops where constructive critiques are used to refine and advance creative material, this workshop will assist students in addressing research challenges through group collaboration and guided discussion of relevant methods and approaches. Level: Graduate

title: Sedimentary Basin Analysis
description: Offered intermittently. Influence of allocyclic processes (tectonism, climate, eustacy, etc.,) in shaping the evolution of sedimentary basins.  Emphasis on integration and synthesis of tools of sedimentary basins analysis, including the study of depositional systems, provenance, paleocurrents, subsidence, sequence stratigraphy, and well logs. Level: Graduate

title: The Food-Energy-Water Nexus
description: Offered autumn. Interdisciplinary course examining interactions between food, energy, and water systems and core concepts and tools at the food-energy-water nexus. Perspectives and connections across scales, sectors, and disciplines (including social and biophysical sciences and engineering) are emphasized. Level: Graduate

title: Seismology and Geodesy
description: Offered Spring. We will explore modern topics in geophysics, with a focus on seismology and geodesy. Advanced topics may vary with each offering, but will generally include selections from continuum mechanics, inverse theory, seismic wave propagation, earthquake location, tidal analysis and prediction, GNSS theory and analysis, spheroidal Earth deformation, and surface mass loading. Students will have the opportunity to engage directly with real seismic and geodetic datasets using computational tools, as well as to investigate problems of personal interest through individual research projects. Level: Graduate

title: Topics in Cryosphere
description: (R-12) Readings, discussions, lectures, and field experiments on various topics related to snow, ice, and climate processes. Recent topics: meltwater infiltration in snow, glacier hydrology, climate cycles, ice, and sea level rise. Level: Graduate

title: Fluvial Geomorphology
description: Offered spring. Application of fluid mechanics to sediment transport and development of river morphology.  Form and process in river meanders, the pool–riffle sequence, aggradation, grade, and baselevel. Level: Graduate

title: Climate Sensitivity and Feedbacks
description: Offered spring. Earth’s climate system response to global radiation imbalances caused by natural and anthropogenic forcings. Fundamentals of climate dynamics including earth’s energy balance and the global circulation of heat; the numerous amplifying and dampening feedbacks governing fast and slow climate system response to forcing; and, the various estimates of earth’s transient response and equilibrium sensitivity to increased atmospheric carbon dioxide concentration. Lectures, readings, discussions, small problem sets and projects. Level: Graduate

title: Advanced Hydrogeology
description: Offered autumn. Advanced concepts used in groundwater investigations, including flow systems analysis, hydrogeologic monitoring and sampling, resource evaluation, exploration, development and monitoring, and contaminant transport. Special problem areas in groundwater exploration and management. Level: Graduate

title: Topics in Structure & Geophysics
description: (R-12) Offerings on request of graduate students by arrangement with appropriate faculty. Recent topics: structural analysis, Precambrian crustal evolution, field trips on Rocky Mountain structure. Level: Graduate

title: Topics in Hydro Low-Temp Geochemistry
description: (R-12) Offerings on request of graduate students by arrangement with appropriate faculty. Recent topics: field methods, well design, contaminant transport, geochemical modeling. Level: Graduate

title: Elementary German I
description: Offered autumn. Emphasis on oral communication, with development in all major skill areas: listening, speaking, reading, and writing.

title: Elementary German II
description: Offered spring. Prereq., GRMN 101 . Emphasis on oral communication, with continuing development in all major skill areas: listening, speaking, reading, and writing.

title: Introduction to German Culture and Civilization
description: This course provides an introductory overview of major developments, ideas, and influences involving German-speaking culture from its documented origins in the Roman era to today in English. Students will become familiar with the chronology and significance of key historical events in Central Europe as well as with major figures in such areas as politics, literature, art, and philosophy. Attention will also be given to important contributions that German-speaking culture has made globally.

title: Special Topics
description: (R–9) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one–time offerings of current topics.

title: Internship

title: Intermediate German I
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., GRMN 102 . Continuation of active skills approach to German listening, speaking, reading, and writing.

title: Intermediate German II
description: Offered spring. Prereq., GRMN 201 . Continuation of 201.

title: Special Topics
description: (R–9) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one–time offerings of current topics.

title: Studies in German Language, Media, and Culture I
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., GRMN 202 . Native or near–native speakers of German may not apply credit for this course toward a German major or minor. The course emphasizes advanced development of the four linguistic skills: listening comprehension, reading, writing, speaking. Interactive approaches toward texts and media foster active participation for conversation. Focus is on development of communication skills, linguistic accuracy, and cross-cultural competence.

title: Studies in German Language, Media, and Culture II
description: Offered spring. Prereq., GRMN 301 . Native or near–native speakers of German may not apply credit for this course toward a German major or minor. This course builds on GRMN 301 and further advances development of the four linguistic skills: listening comprehension, reading, writing, speaking. Use of different types of texts and media extending from GRMN 301 aims toward enhancing cross-cultural competency and proficiency for communication in spoken and written German.

title: Introduction to German Literature
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., GRMN 202 . Reading and discussion of selected works of German literature. Instruction in the fundamentals of textual analysis and terminology and discussion of works in historical context. Taught primarily in German.

title: German Narratives in Literature and Film
description: Prereq., GRMN 202 . In this course we will explore German narratives in literature and film and examine the relationship between literary and cinematic forms in the German-language tradition. We will discuss different aspects of genre and style in German literature and film and how the topics we cover represent developments in Central European literature and culture from the early 19th century to the present. We will also focus on terms and aspects connected to both German literature and cinema. The course will be taught primarily in German.

title: Introduction to Multicultural Literature in Contemporary Germany
description: Offered intermittently. Introduction to multicultural literature created during recent decades in Germany. Study topics include immigration, citizenship, multilingualism, identity; significant literary and cultural movements and selected writers in contemporary Germany.

title: Survey of German Cinema
description: Offered intermittently. The development of the German film from its beginnings in the late 19th century to the present. Topics include Expressionism, New Objectivity, the Nazi film, the German contribution to Hollywood, the post–war film in East and West Germany, and film in unified Germany.

title: Nature and the Environment in German Literature and Film
description: An examination of the historical role of nature and the environment in the German literary and cinematic traditions. Course begins with the Roman Germanic periods and covers literary and cinematic works in cultural and historical context up until the present day. Attention given to the role of environmentalism in Central European culture today in light of themes of nature and the environment in German literature and film historically.

title: German Culture & Civilization
description: Offered spring. Offered as part of the Study Abroad program in Germany and Austria.  Introduction to cultural topics, current events, and historical topics of Germany and Austria. Course content supplemented with on-site visits.

title: German Culture: Beginnings to Romanticism
description: Offered intermittently in spring. Prereq., WRIT 101 or WRIT 201 . Overview of major events and currents in German culture to 1900 with emphasis on the arts, literature, and intellectual movements. Lectures in English.

title: Germ Culture: Romanticism to the Present
description: Offered intermittently in spring. Prereq., WRIT 101 or WRIT 201 . Overview of major events and trends in culture of German-speaking world from 1800 to the present with emphasis on the arts, literature, film, intellectual movements, and social and political developments. Lectures in English.

title: German Literature 1760-1832
description: Offered autumn. Readings, study, and discussion of writers, texts, and contexts in German literature from 1760 to 1832, including Enlightenment, Storm and Stress, Romanticism, and Classicism. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

description: Offered autumn. Readings, study, and discussion of writers, texts, and contexts in German literature from 1832 to 1900. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

description: Prereq., WRIT 101 or WRIT 201 . Western Civilization in Antiquity - Greek, Roman, Jewish and early Christian literatures.

title: Introduction to the Humanities Medieval to Modern
description: Prereq., WRIT 101 or WRIT 201 . Western Civilization in Modernity – early modern (Renaissance) to twentieth century.

title: Global Challenges & Leadership
description: Restricted to students in the Global Leadership Certificate program. Introduction to key global challenges, identifying issues inherent in the challenges, and working with others to research and tackle the challenges.

title: Seminar
description: Offered autumn. Restricted to students in the Global Leadership Certificate program. Small seminar-style courses focused on multidisciplinary approaches to new and enduring global challenges that include intercultural or international perspectives taught by UM faculty around campus.

title: Models of Leadership
description: Restricted to students in the Global Leadership Certificate program. Exploration of leadership models, how culture influences the effectiveness of those models, and how one's leadership style can best be identified, developed, and put into action.

description: (R-3) Restricted to students with senior standing. Culminating project addressing a global question, to include a written proposal (completed and presented in semester 1) and execution of a project (completed and presented in semester 2). Level: Undergraduate

title: Digital Imaging I
description: Offered autumn and spring. Introduction to the fundamentals of digital imaging and composition principles, primarily using Adobe Photoshop. Emphasis on formal 2D design components, typography, and digital compositing. Consideration of historical and contemporary approaches.

title: Graphic Design Applications
description: Offered spring. Prereq., GDSN 149A or MART 255 or MART 256 . This course teaches students to plan and execute visual communication according to the needs of audiences in specific contexts. A thorough examination of the principles of design, theories of communication, and strategies for problem solving will be presented.

title: Elementary Greek I
description: Offered autumn. Introduction to Classical Greek is the first courses of a two-semester sequence, designed to enable the student to read Greek authors in the original Greek as soon as possible. Based upon selected texts from Tragedians, Plato, Xenophon, Menander, New Testament, and other major authors.

title: Elementary Greek II
description: Offered spring. Prereq., GRK 101 . Continuation of GRK 101 . Greek grammar, vocabulary, readings of ancient Greek writings with the aid of a lexicon.

description: (R-6) Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one-time offerings of current topics.

title: Intermediate Greek I
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., GRK 102 . Attic prose and poetry,  Plato, Thucydides, Euripides.

title: Intermediate Greek II
description: Offered spring. Prereq., GRK 201 . Readings from Homer's Iliad and/or Odyssey.

title: Independent Study
description: (R–6) Offered autumn and spring. Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student.

title: Special Topics
description: (R–9) Offered intermittently. Experimental offerings of visiting professors, experimental offerings of new courses, or one–time offerings of current topics.

title: Independent Study
description: (R–9) Offered intermittently. Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student.

title: Independent Study
description: (R–9) Offered intermittently. Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Personal Health and Wellness
description: Focus on health principles and their relevance in contemporary society, the evaluation and application of scientific advances to hypothetical lifestyles, and on contemporary problems in life.

title: Independent Study
description: (R-6) Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student.

title: Peer Health Education
description: Offered fall and spring. Introduction to peer health education strategies and techniques. Students gain the knowledge and skills necessary to develop and implement health promotion programs focused on prevention of major health problems among college students.

title: Peer Health Practicum
description: (R-6) Prereq. or coreq., HTH 370 . Practical experience in planning, coordinating, and implementing health education activities for the campus community. Students address topics related to wellness, drug and alcohol prevention, or sexual assault awareness.

title: Health and Mind/Body/Spirit
description: Restricted to students with at least junior standing. Overview of how the mind/body/spirit relationship affects health. Examination of current research exploring how thoughts, emotions, attitudes, and beliefs influence and mediate health outcome. Exploration of the theoretical applications of mind/body/spirit in health and healing used in contemporary society. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Leading Integrative Physiology and Athletic Training Organizations
description: Prereq., KIN 205 . Restricted to students with at least junior standing. Leadership, management, organizational structure assertiveness, conflict management, public relations, decision-making, budget management, and a broad overview of human resource management, all as they relate to health and human performance settings. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Legal and Ethical Issues Health and Exercise Professions
description: Restricted to students with at least junior standing. Legal  bases for litigation in the health and exercise professions, with emphasis on negligence, liability, and risk identification and risk management. Utilizing the Western ethical traditions, the ethics component examines  moral/ethical development through the lifespan via analysis of specific human  behaviors. Level: Undergraduate-Graduate

title: Teaching HHP
description: (R-4) Students assist in the preparation and grading of demonstrations and laboratory assignments, and laboratory instruction of undergraduate students enrolled in HHP laboratory courses. Students are given advanced instruction in principles of the HHP course. Level: Undergraduate

title: Independent Study
description: (R-6) Course material appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student. Level: Undergraduate

title: Seminar
description: (R-2) Co-req., HTH 395 and CHTH 498 . Health Behavior Coach practicum and internship students enrolled in this seminar will have the opportunity to critically analyze their coach/client interactions and to receive feedback from faculty and peers regarding their coaching skills. Students' coaching skills will be enhanced and strengthened through assigned readings and skill building activities. CR/NCR. Level: Undergraduate

title: Internship
description: (R-6) Restricted to students with at least junior standing. Extended classroom experience that provides practical application of classroom learning during placements off campus. Prior approval must be obtained from the faculty supervisor. A maximum of 6 credits of Internship (198, 298, 398, 498) may count toward graduation. Level: Undergraduate

title: Research Methods
description: Offered every term. An introduction to research design and methodologies in integrative physiology, athletic training, and related fields. The course is designed to explore the consumption of research literature, study design, methodology, and the associated data analyses and interpretation as related to hypothesis-driven research. Students will learn the foundational approaches to modern research in order to become better consumers of research in the field, and enable their own exploration in research design. Level: Graduate

title: Case Studies in Performance Psychology
description: Offered intermittently. Restricted to students in the Master of Athletic Training (MAT) program. Through the usage of both real and hypothetical case studies, the course will examine the field of sport/performance psychology and its role in the broader field of sports medicine. Level: Graduate

title: Ethics & Human Performance
description: A critical examination of the ethical issues dominating the field of health and human performance and beyond with special emphasis on developing the conceptual frameworks needed to articulate our concerns and engage in meaningful dialogue with others. Level: Graduate

title: Advanced Biomechanics
description: This course is focused on developing laboratory skills and an advanced understanding of the quantitative and qualitative basis for human motion.  Particular emphasis will be placed on the Newtonian mechanics governing biological motion and the roles of the musculo-skeletal, nervous and cardio-vascular systems during human activity.  This integrative approach will be used to quantify and understand motion by, and within, the human body; examples will be drawn from the sub-disciplines of clinical gait analysis, gerontology, sports medicine, biological engineering and performance physiology.  The lecture portion of this course is co-convened with KIN 425 Biomechanics. Level: Graduate

title: Higher Education Pedagogy in Integrative Sciences
description: This course discusses the science behind student learning, scientific teaching, assessments and rubrics, active learning, project based learning, teaching technology, inclusive teaching and universal design, classroom and course management, and course design. Level: Graduate

title: Advanced Exercise Prescription
description: Offered spring even years. This course presents the principles and practices of advanced athletic performance training in a thorough and useful sequence.  Testing and improving power, strength, speed, quickness, coordination, agility, flexibility, local muscular endurance, and cardiovascular aerobic capacity and endurance are covered based on the scientific record. Students will learn how to tailor sport specific training exercises and drills and periodize the training program precisely for peak performance at critical points in the competitive season. Level: Graduate

title: Advanced Exercise Physiology I
description: Offered autumn. Prereq., KIN 320 or KIN 321 . Advanced study of the effect of work, activity and exercise on human biochemistry, metabolism, endocrinology and muscle function. Level: Graduate

title: Advanced Exercise Physiology II
description: Offered spring odd years. Prereq., HHP 529 . Advanced study of system physiology (circulatory, respiratory and renal function) and environmental factors applied to physical work, activity and exercise Level: Graduate

title: Lab Procedures In Exercise Science
description: Offered autumn. Introduction to common laboratory tools associated with clinical and health assessment techniques, research measures, and data collection. Level: Graduate

title: Advanced Electrocardiogram Assessment
description: Students should have completed an undergraduate course in exercise physiology. Evaluation in the form of a traditional letter grade will be based on performance on exams, on-line quizzes, a lab practical, lab practical mentoring, a topical paper and presentation. Level: Graduate

title: Teaching Health & Human Performance
description: (R-9) Offered fall and spring. Students assist with the preparation of lecture demonstrations, the grading of laboratory assignments, and the delivery of content, analysis, and safety protocols for the undergraduate laboratory sections to which they are assigned. The weekly meetings with the instructor of record provide students with advanced instruction in the relevant physiology and ensure agreement of objectives across all sections of the specific undergraduate course. Level: Graduate

title: Advanced Exercise, Disease, and Aging
description: Prereq., KIN 460 or HHP 560 . Students should have completed an undergraduate course in exercise physiology. Course performance will include the assignment of traditional letter grades based upon exams, on line quizzes, a topical paper and presentation, and mentoring of undergraduate peer-teaching. Level: Graduate

title: Laboratory for Advanced Exercise, Disease, and Aging
description: Prereq., KIN 460 or HHP 560 . Students should have completed an undergraduate course in exercise physiology. This course is designed for IPAT masters students and doctoral students in the proposed Integrative Physiology and Rehabilitation Sciences programs. Students will be evaluated using a traditional letter grade as assessed using lab practical assignments, an exam, and performance on team leading human subjects exercise testing risk assessments, exercise testing and interpretation, client counseling for exercise prescriptions and cardiovascular and metabolic disease risk modification using a 'lifestyle medicine' approach. Level: Graduate

description: (R-3) Offered every term. Prereq., HHP 520 . Preparation of a professional paper appropriate to the needs and objectives of the individual student. Level: Graduate

title: Research
description: (R-6) Offered every term. Directed individual research and study appropriate to the background and objectives of the student. Level: Graduate

title: Thesis/Dissertation
description: (R-18) Option for the proposed doctoral in Integrative Physiology and Rehabilitation Sciences. Work will reflect a research project designed by the doctoral candidate, their doctoral committee chair and with input from the doctoral committee members. Level: Graduate

title: Professional Activities I
description: The instruction of basic skills for tennis, basketball, and Western Swing. Techniques, drills, and strategies will be taught. Demonstration and instruction skills developed. Active participation required.

title: Professional Activities II
description: The instruction of basic skills for soccer, volleyball, and golf. Techniques, drills, and strategies will be taught. Demonstration and instruction skills developed. Active participation required.
